concise:
Ayah 1–18 · linked video: 01. Al-Anbya (Ayah 1-18)

Surah Al-Anbiya: The Prophets

Ayah 1

ٱقْتَرَبَ لِلنَّاسِ حِسَابُهُمْ وَهُمْ فِى غَفْلَةٍ مُّعْرِضُونَ

The accounting for people has drawn very near, and they are in complete heedlessness, deliberately turning away. Allah ﷻ opens this Surah with a stark warning, setting the tone for what follows. When Allah ﷻ says the reckoning has approached - that it has come very, very near - He establishes an atmosphere of urgency and warning.

The word اقْتَرَبَ communicates not just nearness, but immediate proximity. Yet despite this closeness of their accounting, humanity remains فِى غَفْلَةٍ مُّعْرِضُونَ - in complete heedlessness and deliberately ignoring. This is the “I don’t care” attitude, the “whatever” mentality that characterizes those who refuse to take the reality of their return to Allah ﷻ seriously.

Ayah 2

مَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّن رَّبِّهِم مُّحْدَثٍ إِلَّا ٱسْتَمَعُوهُ وَهُمْ يَلْعَبُونَ

Whenever any new reminder comes to them from their Master, they listen to it while they are playing around. The complaint continues: not only are they heedless, but when any reminder does reach them - any new revelation - they pretend to listen carefully while treating it as entertainment. The word يَلْعَبُونَ means they are literally playing, not taking it seriously.

For those reading this who might be distracted by their phones while listening to reminders of Allah ﷻ, reflect on this description. They’re listening while playing - giving partial attention while their hearts remain engaged in amusement rather than contemplation.

Ayah 3

لَاهِيَةً قُلُوبُهُمْ ۗ وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَى ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ هَلْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ ۖ أَفَتَأْتُونَ ٱلسِّحْرَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ

Their hearts are distracted. Those who do wrong hold secret conversations: “Is this not just a human being like yourselves? Will you succumb to magic while you can see clearly?”

The word لَاهِيَةً describes hearts that are perpetually distracted, filled with entertainment and play. Lahw refers to sitting around telling stories, playing games, engaging in amusement - hearts that never get serious about the weightier matters of existence.

But something interesting happens. Sometimes even these distracted hearts get struck by a particular statement from the revelation. It hits them. In those moments, they retreat to their private councils - وَأَسَرُّوا۟ ٱلنَّجْوَى (they keep their conversations secret). One person might say, “That thing he said really got to me. Could he actually be a messenger?” But immediately, the others pull him back: “Come on, don’t be weak. Don’t take this seriously. He’s making it up.”

Those who did wrong - ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ - arrive at their final conclusion through these secret discussions: “Isn’t he just a human being like yourselves?” This is their ultimate dismissal. He’s nothing special, just an ordinary man. Why are you so impressed?

Then, if someone shows continued interest, the company intensifies their efforts: “Are you being affected by magic? Are you caving into sorcery while you can see clearly with your own eyes?” The implication is that the message’s effectiveness is merely magical deception, and the person considering it is being weak-minded.

This captures the immense power of company. Sometimes the Prophet ﷺ would hear of someone who had been considering Islam, coming close to accepting it, only to have his friends pull him away with these very arguments: “This is just magic. Don’t worry about it. You’re stronger than this.”

Ayah 4

قَالَ رَبِّى يَعْلَمُ ٱلْقَوْلَ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ وَهُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

He said, “My Master knows whatever is said in the heavens and the earth. And He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.”

These private conversations they thought were secret - Allah ﷻ responds that He knows every word uttered in the skies and the earth. These supposedly private councils where they convince each other to reject the message, where they pull people away from considering Islam - nothing is hidden from Allah ﷻ. He is السَّمِيعُ - the All-Hearing who hears everything, and الْعَلِيمُ - the All-Knowing who knows the reality behind every statement.

Ayah 5

بَلْ قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍۭ بَلِ ٱفْتَرَىٰهُ بَلْ هُوَ شَاعِرٌ فَلْيَأْتِنَا بِـَٔايَةٍ كَمَآ أُرْسِلَ ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ

Rather, they say, “These are confused dreams.” No, “He has invented it.” No, “He is a poet.” Let him bring us a sign like those sent to the former peoples.

Notice the rapid shifting of accusations. The word أَضْغَـٰثُ refers to confused, mixed-up speech - like nightmares. In old Arabic, when someone narrates a dream with uncertainty, saying “I don’t know what I saw… I think it was like a cow or something eating other cows,” that confused retelling is called adghath. They’re saying, “What he’s telling us is all based on crazy nightmares he’s had. He’s lost his mind.”

But if that accusation doesn’t stick, they immediately pivot: “No, no - he didn’t see nightmares. He made this up. He’s a creative writer. He invented all of this.”

And if someone still seems impressed despite that accusation, they shift again: “Well, he’s a poet. He’s really talented with words, that’s all.”

Each accusation is a different strategy to prevent people from taking the message seriously. If one doesn’t work, move to the next. This is the methodology of those who oppose truth - keep throwing different accusations until something sticks.

Finally, they make their ultimate demand: “Let him bring us a miracle like the earlier messengers were sent with.” They want to see the staff turn into a serpent like Musa (AS), or the ocean part, or some other spectacular miracle.

Ayah 6

مَآ ءَامَنَتْ قَبْلَهُم مِّن قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ ۖ أَفَهُمْ يُؤْمِنُونَ

Not a single town that We destroyed before them believed. Will these people then believe?

This is Allah’s response to their demand for miracles. The devastating answer: not one nation before them that witnessed such miracles actually believed. Those spectacular signs didn’t produce faith in previous communities. The pattern throughout history has been consistent - when miracles come and people still reject, destruction follows immediately. There’s no second chance after the miracle.

Allah ﷻ is showing mercy by not sending those miracles, because He knows how this audience will respond. He knows they won’t submit, and sending the miracle would only seal their fate. They’re essentially asking for their own destruction without realizing it.

Ayah 7

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا قَبْلَكَ إِلَّا رِجَالًا نُّوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَسْـَٔلُوٓا۟ أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ إِن كُنتُمْ لَا تَعْلَمُونَ

We never sent before you except men to whom We revealed. So ask the People of the Scripture if you do not know.

Their complaint that Muhammad ﷺ is “just a man” receives a direct response. Allah ﷻ declares that He has never sent anyone except رِجَالًا - men, human beings just like them - to whom He revealed. This has been the consistent pattern throughout all of prophetic history.

If they doubt this, Allah ﷻ tells them to go ask أَهْلَ ٱلذِّكْرِ - the People of Remembrance, meaning the Jews and Christians who possess the Torah and Gospel. Ask them: Were the messengers in your scriptures walking around in markets? Did they eat food? Did they sleep and live like normal human beings? The answer they’ll receive is yes - every single messenger was fully human, living an ordinary human life in that respect.

This complaint about the Prophet ﷺ being human is not new. It’s ignorance of the entire history of prophethood. When people don’t know anything about how Allah ﷻ has always sent His guidance, they should consult those who do have that knowledge.

Ayah 8

وَمَا جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ جَسَدًا لَّا يَأْكُلُونَ ٱلطَّعَامَ وَمَا كَانُوا۟ خَـٰلِدِينَ

We did not give them bodies that did not eat food, nor were they immortal.

Allah ﷻ continues demolishing their objection. The prophets were not given supernatural bodies that didn’t require food. They weren’t made خَـٰلِدِينَ - eternal or immortal. They experienced hunger, needed sustenance, and eventually died like all human beings. This is the nature of messengerhood that Allah ﷻ has always established.

Ayah 9

ثُمَّ صَدَقْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْوَعْدَ فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُمْ وَمَن نَّشَآءُ وَأَهْلَكْنَا ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ

Then We fulfilled Our promise to them, and We saved them and whoever We willed, and We destroyed those who exceeded all bounds.

The pattern with previous messengers was consistent. Allah ﷻ promised them that if their people didn’t believe, the time of reckoning would come. When communities failed to reform, Allah ﷻ fulfilled His promise - صَدَقْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْوَعْدَ (We made true Our promise to them).

The messengers were rescued - فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُمْ - along with whoever Allah ﷻ willed from among the believers. But those who were مُسْرِفِينَ - those who went overboard, who exceeded all limits in their transgression - were destroyed. Israf means to go beyond bounds, to transgress limits that should never be crossed.

Ayah 10

لَقَدْ أَنزَلْنَآ إِلَيْكُمْ كِتَـٰبًا فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

We have certainly sent down to you a Book in which is your mention. Will you not use reason?

This is one of the Quran’s most powerful statements about its own relevance. فِيهِ ذِكْرُكُمْ - “In it is your dhikr.” This phrase carries two profound meanings.

First, this Book contains your story. When you read about Ibrahim (AS) struggling with his father, you should see your own struggles with your parents. When you learn about the trials of Yusuf (AS) in his youth, facing temptation, you should recognize your own battles with desire. The Quran isn’t ancient history disconnected from your life - it’s talking about you, your challenges, your journey.

Second, this is the dhikr - the remembrance of Allah ﷻ - that has been custom-made for you. This is how you are meant to remember your Lord.

Then comes the challenge: أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ - “Will you not use your intellect?” Will you not exercise reason and see how directly relevant this guidance is to your own life?

Ayah 11

وَكَمْ قَصَمْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ كَانَتْ ظَالِمَةً وَأَنشَأْنَا بَعْدَهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ

How many towns that were unjust have We shattered and raised up after them another people!

The word قَصَمْنَا is intensely violent - it means to crush, to mangle, to smash to pieces, to dismember. This isn’t gentle destruction; this is complete obliteration. How many wrongdoing nations - كَانَتْ ظَالِمَةً - has Allah ﷻ utterly destroyed in this manner?

And after their destruction, وَأَنشَأْنَا بَعْدَهَا قَوْمًا ءَاخَرِينَ - We raised up after them another people entirely. The message is clear: Don’t think you’re irreplaceable. It’s not difficult for Allah ﷻ to completely wipe you out and bring forth a new nation. Who do you think you’re playing with?

Ayah 12

فَلَمَّآ أَحَسُّوا۟ بَأْسَنَآ إِذَا هُم مِّنْهَا يَرْكُضُونَ

Then when they perceived Our punishment, at once they fled from it.

When these wrongdoing nations finally sensed - أَحَسُّوا۟ - that Allah’s punishment was coming, when the people of Lut (AS), the people of Salih (AS), the people of Shu’ayb (AS) realized destruction was upon them, immediately - إِذَا - they fled.

The word يَرْكُضُونَ literally means hitting one’s heels. In old Arabic, when you rode a horse and wanted it to run, you would strike your heels against it. The imagery is of people desperately trying to escape, running from their towns as fast as they possibly can. They knew there was no place safe to stay anymore.

Ayah 13

لَا تَرْكُضُوا۟ وَٱرْجِعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰ مَآ أُتْرِفْتُمْ فِيهِ وَمَسَـٰكِنِكُمْ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْـَٔلُونَ

“Do not flee, but return to where you were given luxury and to your homes, so that you may be questioned.”

Then comes the divine command, dripping with sarcasm: Don’t run. Go back to مَآ أُتْرِفْتُمْ - the luxury you enjoyed, the elite lifestyle you lived. The word إِتْرَاف refers to having abundant wealth and enjoying it lavishly, living in comfort and extravagance.

In modern terms, this is like someone getting into their luxury car as the earthquake begins, the flood starts, or the meteor shower commences, and Allah ﷻ takes control of the steering wheel, turns the car around, and says: “No, no, no. Go back to your mansion that’s collapsing on your head.”

وَمَسَـٰكِنِكُمْ - Go back to your homes, لَعَلَّكُمْ تُسْـَٔلُونَ - so that you can be questioned. This can mean questioned on Judgment Day, but it also carries bitter irony in the immediate context: Go back to your servants who constantly asked, “Can I get you some water? Can I get you something to drink?” Go back to those who catered to your every whim. Why don’t you head back there now?

Ayah 14

قَالُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ

They cried, “Woe to us! Indeed, we have been wrongdoers!”

As the destruction unfolds around them, they finally cry out: يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ - what terrible destruction has fallen upon us! إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ - we were the ones who were wrong all this time. We were the criminals. We were the oppressors.

The houses are collapsing, the earth is sinking, destruction is happening all around them, and finally - finally - they recognize what the messenger had been telling them. They see it clearly now: “This is exactly what he warned us about. We were wrong. We messed up.”

Ayah 15

فَمَا زَالَت تِّلْكَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ حَتَّىٰ جَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ حَصِيدًا خَـٰمِدِينَ

That continued to be their cry until We made them like a mowed-down field, totally silenced.

They kept crying this way - فَمَا زَالَت تِّلْكَ دَعْوَىٰهُمْ - this remained their continuous cry, until Allah ﷻ made them حَصِيدًا.

Haseed refers to a field after the harvest, after the tractor has gone over it, or after farmers have cut through it with machetes. What remains on the ground are just broken stalks - remnants that show a plant used to be here, but now it’s torn off, destroyed. This is what they looked like: you could tell there used to be human beings here, but now only remains are visible.

خَـٰمِدِينَ is even more chilling. Khamada refers to when a fever breaks, when intense heat begins to cool. It’s used for burning coal that slowly loses its heat and starts turning gray, the life in it dying out. It’s used as a figure of speech for a slow, painful death - like watching embers gradually lose their glow until they become cold ash.

Until they became like cut crops, and the life in them was withering away like cooling coals - this is the horrific end that came to them.

Ayah 16

وَمَا خَلَقْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَـٰعِبِينَ

We did not create the heavens and the earth and everything between them as a game.

Remember where this Surah began - with people treating the message as play, as entertainment, as something not to be taken seriously. Now Allah ﷻ declares definitively: We did not create the heavens and the earth لَـٰعِبِينَ - playfully, as a game.

This addresses a claim you might encounter, especially in academic settings, where people suggest, “What if God is just messing around with us? What if He created all of this just to play with us, to toy with us through earthquakes and disasters? Maybe it’s all just a game to Him.”

The Quran’s response is unequivocal: This creation is not a game. Existence has profound purpose and meaning.

Ayah 17

لَوْ أَرَدْنَآ أَن نَّتَّخِذَ لَهْوًا لَّٱتَّخَذْنَـٰهُ مِن لَّدُنَّآ إِن كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ

If We had intended to take a pastime, We would have taken it from Our own presence - if We were to do such a thing at all.

If Allah ﷻ wanted to take لَهْوًا - entertainment or pastime - He would have taken it مِن لَّدُنَّآ - from Our own presence, from Our own domain. The implication is that Allah ﷻ has no need for creation as entertainment. If He wanted amusement, He wouldn’t need to create an entire universe for it.

But the statement ends with إِن كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ - “if We were to do such a thing at all.” The conditional itself questions the premise. This isn’t something Allah ﷻ would do in the first place. The entire suggestion that creation is divine entertainment is absurd from its foundation.

Ayah 18

بَلْ نَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَى ٱلْبَـٰطِلِ فَيَدْمَغُهُۥ فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ ۚ وَلَكُمُ ٱلْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ

Rather, We hurl the truth against falsehood, and it crushes its head, and falsehood vanishes instantly. And woe to you for what you describe!

This is one of the most powerful images in the Quran regarding the battle between truth and falsehood. بَلْ - rather, the reality is this: نَقْذِفُ بِٱلْحَقِّ عَلَى ٱلْبَـٰطِلِ - We take the truth and We hurl it against falsehood.

The word نَقْذِفُ creates a vivid scene. Imagine someone running away, and their pursuer can’t catch them, so he grabs a rock or a spear and hurls it with tremendous force. The imagery is of something thrown so hard that فَيَدْمَغُهُۥ - it bashes the skull, it strikes the head with such impact that it crushes the brain.

Now picture truth and falsehood as two persons, enemies of each other. One person hates another, so he yells at him. Another hates someone else, so he punches him. Another hates so much he beats him up, or stabs him. But this hatred between truth and falsehood is so intense that truth doesn’t just confront falsehood - it hurls something that strikes falsehood’s head and destroys its very brain. It’s a graphic, disturbing image that captures how much truth cannot tolerate falsehood.

They cannot exist in the same room together. Truth and falsehood maintain distance from one another - the word قَذْف itself implies distance, something thrown from far away. They can’t even be close to one another.

This teaches us something critical: most propaganda is truth mixed with falsehood. But the Quran shows us that truth cannot tolerate even a little bit of falsehood. It has zero tolerance for it, and it must destroy it completely.

فَإِذَا هُوَ زَاهِقٌ - then falsehood is زَاهِقٌ. This word beautifully captures defeat. Zahaq is used when someone runs away in defeat from a battlefield - struck once and realizing “I can’t deal with this fight,” then fleeing. It’s also used for when the soul departs the body, as mentioned in Surah At-Tawbah.

What makes zahiq particularly rich is that it’s used for two types of wild animals: either extremely fat animals or extremely skinny ones - like cows or goats. Why? Because these are the animals that, when they see a predator, they run. But the fat one can’t get very far because of its weight, and the skinny one isn’t strong enough to run far, and both get caught.

So the imagery shifts: from someone bashing another’s head to a predator pursuing helpless prey. Falsehood is that helpless prey, and truth is the predator that inevitably catches it.

وَلَكُمُ ٱلْوَيْلُ مِمَّا تَصِفُونَ - And woe to you for the characterizations you make, for the descriptions you fabricate. For those who describe Allah ﷻ and His revelation falsely, for those who characterize the Prophet ﷺ as a magician or a madman or a poet, for those who paint falsehood as truth and truth as falsehood - ultimate destruction awaits them.

Ayah 19–33 · linked video: 02. Al-Anbya (Ayah 19-33)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 19-33

Ayah 19

وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۚ وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ

And to Him belongs whoever is in the heavens and the earth. And those who are near Him are not too proud to worship Him, nor do they grow weary.

Allah ﷻ establishes His complete ownership over all creation. The phrase وَلَهُۥ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ declares that He owns entirely and exclusively whoever exists in the heavens and the earth. This is followed by a special mention of وَمَنْ عِندَهُۥ - those who are in His company, referring to the angels and those slaves who are closest to Him.

The ayah then describes their relationship with worship: لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ عَنْ عِبَادَتِهِۦ - they are not too arrogant to show their slavery to Him. The word عِبَادَة encompasses both worship and slavery, and slavery represents the most humiliating position a human being can occupy, just as mastery represents the highest status. Consider how people view certain positions as beneath them - someone might refuse a lower job position because they feel it’s beneath their dignity. Yet those who are closest to Allah ﷻ, the angels and His honored slaves, don’t show even a trace of arrogance in taking the position of slave before Him. They have no pride that prevents them from complete submission.

The second part, وَلَا يَسْتَحْسِرُونَ, tells us they don’t get lazy as a result of exhaustion. The root حسر means exhaustion - the Quran uses this word elsewhere when describing eyes exhausted from searching for cracks in the sky. Here, Allah ﷻ tells us that these honored beings don’t become lazy despite any exhaustion. They maintain their worship continuously, never allowing fatigue to diminish their devotion.

Ayah 20

يُسَبِّحُونَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ لَا يَفْتُرُونَ

They glorify [Him] night and day, never slackening.

This ayah expands on the previous description, showing us how these beings maintain their worship. يُسَبِّحُونَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ - they declare Allah’s perfection night and day, without interruption.

The word لَا يَفْتُرُونَ is particularly significant. فُتُور describes that specific kind of laziness where someone starts with tremendous energy but then tapers off. Think of someone who begins Salah with great focus and enthusiasm, but by the second rakah their energy has completely dissipated. Or consider those who used to be incredibly active in their worship, making extra prayers and demonstrating deep devotion, but over time that enthusiasm has waned. That’s فُتُور - starting strong and then losing steam.

Allah ﷻ tells us that these honored beings are not like that. They don’t have seasonal devotion. They don’t start off energetic and then slow down. Their worship maintains the same level of dedication and focus continuously, night and day, without any reduction in their commitment.

Ayah 21

أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ هُمْ يُنشِرُونَ

Or have they taken gods from the earth who resurrect [the dead]?

After describing the perfect worship of those who truly understand Allah’s majesty, the Quran now turns to the absurdity of those who worship false gods. أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ - or have these disbelievers taken gods out of the earth?

The phrase هُمْ يُنشِرُونَ is deeply ironic. يُنشِرُونَ comes from the root meaning to raise, to take care of, to distribute. These people have made gods that they themselves must raise up, take care of, and protect. They have to repaint them when the paint chips away, re-carve them when they get damaged, protect them from hail damage when they’re placed outside, wash the rust off metal idols. The supposed “gods” require constant maintenance from their worshippers. They also have to distribute these idols, carrying them from place to place, setting them up in different locations. The worshippers are taking care of their gods rather than the gods taking care of them - a complete reversal of what divinity should mean.

Ayah 22

لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَآ ءَالِهَةٌ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا ۚ فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ

Had there been within them [i.e., the heavens and earth] gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined. So exalted is Allah, Lord of the Throne, above what they describe.

This ayah presents a logical proof of Allah’s oneness. لَوْ كَانَ فِيهِمَآ ءَالِهَةٌ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ لَفَسَدَتَا - if there had been in the heavens or the earth any gods besides Allah, both would have been corrupted. The word فَسَد means corruption, ruin, disorder.

The logic is clear: multiple gods would inevitably lead to conflict. This is why every religious mythology that includes multiple deities invariably includes stories of fights and battles between those gods. Whether you examine Greek mythology, Hindu mythology, or mythologies from across the world, you find the same pattern - gods fighting each other for supremacy. Zeus sits on top, but his sons constantly attempt to overthrow him. The gods scheme against each other, form alliances, wage wars. This is the natural consequence of claiming multiple ultimate powers.

The ayah concludes with فَسُبْحَـٰنَ ٱللَّهِ رَبِّ ٱلْعَرْشِ - how perfect and far above is Allah, the Master of the Throne. There is only one throne, and there is only one Master. The word سُبْحَـٰنَ captures this perfectly - it means you cannot say something so low about someone so high. Allah ﷻ is far above these characterizations.

The final phrase عَمَّا يَصِفُونَ is also significant. وَصْف in Arabic means to describe something in elaborate detail. When people create these mythologies, they don’t just make simple claims - they develop incredibly elaborate stories. This god has these specific powers, lived in this particular place, moved to another location, had these children, fought these battles. The level of detail in false mythologies is remarkable. Today, these elaborate stories are adapted into movies and entertainment. Even though presented as fiction, these narratives leave residue on the mind. For a believer, even joking about such things - assigning different powers to different imaginary deities - should be offensive. Allah ﷻ is far above all these elaborate descriptions and false attributions.

Ayah 23

لَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْـَٔلُونَ

He is not questioned about what He does, but they will be questioned.

This ayah establishes a fundamental principle: لَا يُسْـَٔلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ - He is not questioned about whatever He does. Allah ﷻ is accountable to no one. No one has the authority to interrogate Him or demand explanations for His actions, decisions, or decrees.

In contrast, وَهُمْ يُسْـَٔلُونَ - they are the ones who will be interrogated. All of creation will stand before Allah ﷻ and answer for their choices and actions. The contrast is absolute: the Creator who answers to no one, and the creation who must answer for everything.

Ayah 24

أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً ۖ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ ۖ هَـٰذَا ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِىَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِى ۗ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ ۖ فَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ

Or have they taken gods besides Him? Say, [O Muhammad], “Produce your proof. This is the message for those with me and the message of those before me.” But most of them do not know the truth, so they are turning away.

The ayah returns to challenging the polytheists: أَمِ ٱتَّخَذُوا۟ مِن دُونِهِۦٓ ءَالِهَةً - did they take gods besides Him? Then comes the command to the Prophet ﷺ: قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ - tell them to bring their proof.

The word هَاتُوا۟ is powerful. It comes from the root meaning “give” but carries an urgent, commanding tone - like someone demanding “Hand it over! Right now!” It’s used when someone is exerting authority and demanding immediate compliance. So Allah ﷻ is telling them: Bring your irrefutable proofs for your mythologies - bring them immediately!

Then Allah ﷻ provides the actual proof: هَـٰذَا ذِكْرُ مَن مَّعِىَ وَذِكْرُ مَن قَبْلِى - this Quran is a mention of whoever is with me and those who came before me. The Quran describes the characteristics of believers and mentions the nations that came before. It provides a complete, coherent message that has remained consistent through all the prophets.

But the reality of the disbelievers is this: بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ ٱلْحَقَّ - most of them don’t even know the truth. And when you don’t know something, what often happens? فَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ - you decide to ignore it. There’s a tragic attitude many people develop: “If I didn’t need to know about this until now, how important can it really be? I’ve survived this far in my life without knowing about Islam, so why do I need to know about it now? I’ll be alright.” This is the essence of إعراض - turning away from truth because you’ve convinced yourself you don’t need it.

Ayah 25

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ فَٱعْبُدُونِ

And We sent not before you any messenger except that We revealed to him that there is no deity except Me, so worship Me.

This ayah establishes the fundamental message that unites all prophets. وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ - We never sent a messenger before you at all, إِلَّا نُوحِىٓ إِلَيْهِ - except that We revealed to him one same truth.

And what is that universal revelation? أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنَا۠ - that there is no one worthy of worship or obedience in any way, shape or form except Myself. This is the core message of every prophet, from Adam (AS) to Muhammad ﷺ. The details of law may have varied, the specific circumstances of each nation differed, but this central truth remained constant.

The ayah concludes with the natural consequence of this truth: فَٱعْبُدُونِ - then enslave yourselves to Me. Once you recognize that there is only one God, the only logical response is complete submission to Him alone.

Ayah 26

وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا ۗ سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ ۚ بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ

And they say, “The Most Merciful has taken a son.” Exalted is He! Rather, they are [but] honored servants.

The ayah addresses a particular form of shirk: وَقَالُوا۟ ٱتَّخَذَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ وَلَدًا - some of them said that Ar-Rahman, the Most Merciful, has taken a son. This refers to various claims made by different groups - some Christians claiming Jesus (AS) is the son of God, some Arab pagans claiming angels are daughters of Allah.

The response is immediate and emphatic: سُبْحَـٰنَهُۥ - how far above He is from that claim! How perfect He is! The use of سُبْحَـٰنَ again emphasizes that this claim is so low, so beneath the reality of who Allah ﷻ is, that it’s almost incomprehensible someone could make it.

Then Allah ﷻ corrects the record: بَلْ عِبَادٌ مُّكْرَمُونَ - rather, the case is that those people they talk about being Allah’s son are actually slaves that were honored. They are عِبَاد (slaves, servants) but they are مُّكْرَمُونَ (honored ones). Their status is high, but it is the status of honored slaves, not children of God.

Ayah 27

لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِۦ يَعْمَلُونَ

They cannot precede Him in word, and they act by His command.

Having established that these beings are honored slaves, the ayah now describes how they honor Allah ﷻ. لَا يَسْبِقُونَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ - they never speak before Allah. They don’t open their mouths until Allah ﷻ gives the instruction. This is a profound show of respect.

Consider the teacher-student relationship. If a student speaks before the teacher, cutting them off to say what they want to say first, that’s disrespectful. The teacher should speak first. But these honored slaves of Allah don’t even open their mouths, don’t even say anything before Him. They wait for His permission, His command, His instruction.

And when He does command them? وَهُم بِأَمْرِهِۦ يَعْمَلُونَ - whatever commands He gives, they follow it, they obey it, they do it. Their obedience is immediate and complete.

Ayah 28

يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِۦ مُشْفِقُونَ

He knows what is [presently] before them and what will be after them, and they cannot intercede except on behalf of one whom He approves. And they, from fear of Him, are apprehensive.

Allah ﷻ knows everything about these honored slaves: يَعْلَمُ مَا بَيْنَ أَيْدِيهِمْ وَمَا خَلْفَهُمْ - He knows what is in front of them and what is behind them, what they’ve done and what lies ahead.

Despite their honored status, their ability to intercede is strictly limited: وَلَا يَشْفَعُونَ إِلَّا لِمَنِ ٱرْتَضَىٰ - they are not going to intercede on anyone’s behalf except for the one He may be pleased with. Even the prophets cannot speak on behalf of just anyone. Their intercession is only by Allah’s permission and only for those He approves.

And why is this? وَهُم مِّنْ خَشْيَتِهِۦ مُشْفِقُونَ - because they themselves, out of fear of Allah, are terrified. They themselves are horrified on the Day of Judgment. When we learn about that Day in the authentic hadith, we see people going to various prophets seeking intercession, and each prophet responds “Nafsi, nafsi” - “Myself, myself! I have nothing to do with you. I’m terrified for myself.” If these honored beings are in such a state of awe and fear, how can they possibly speak on just anyone’s behalf?

Ayah 29

۞ وَمَن يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ إِنِّىٓ إِلَـٰهٌ مِّن دُونِهِۦ فَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

And whoever of them should say, “Indeed, I am a god besides Him” - that one We would recompense with Hell. Thus do We recompense the wrongdoers.

This ayah contains a beautiful grammatical subtlety. Notice the phrase وَمَن يَقُلْ مِنْهُمْ - “whoever of them would say.” The verb يَقُلْ is in a form called majzoom, which typically requires an indicator of why it takes that form. But here, there’s no such indicator visible. When this happens in Arabic grammar, the statement becomes hypothetical rather than actual.

Allah ﷻ has been talking about the prophets and honored slaves who obey Him perfectly, who don’t speak before Him, who act only by His command. Now He says: whoever of them would have said “I am a god besides Him” - note that He doesn’t say “whoever of them says” because none of them actually said it. This can only be stated hypothetically.

The consequence is absolute: فَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِيهِ جَهَنَّمَ - that person, We would compensate him fully with Jahannam. كَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِى ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - that is how We compensate those who do wrong. Even if one of these honored beings were to make such a claim, the punishment would be Hell. This emphasizes the severity of claiming divinity and the absolute impossibility that any true prophet or angel would ever make such a claim.

Ayah 30

أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا فَفَتَقْنَـٰهُمَا ۖ وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ ۖ أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ

Have those who disbelieved not considered that the heavens and the earth were a joined entity, and then We separated them and made from water every living thing? Then will they not believe?

This remarkable ayah shifts to presenting evidence from the natural world. أَوَلَمْ يَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ - didn’t those who disbelieve already observe? The verb يَرَ can mean both “to see” and “to think deeply about.”

أَنَّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ كَانَتَا رَتْقًا - that the heavens and the earth were both fused together. The word رَتْقًا means joined, fused, sewn together. Then فَفَتَقْنَـٰهُمَا - We tore them apart. The root فتق is the direct opposite of رتق, so the contrast is built right into the words themselves.

Some scholars have noted that this description bears resemblance to what modern science calls the Big Bang theory - the idea that the entire universe was once concentrated in a tiny, fused point of massive energy that then burst apart in an explosion, with the universe continuing to expand ever since. The Quran describes the heavens and earth as having been fused together and then torn open.

But the ayah doesn’t stop with the origin of the universe. It also addresses the origin of life: وَجَعَلْنَا مِنَ ٱلْمَآءِ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ حَىٍّ - and We made from water every living thing. This is now unanimously agreed upon in modern science - water is the source of the origin of life. This is why even in space exploration, scientists search for traces of water, knowing that where there’s water, there may be life.

What’s significant here is that Allah ﷻ is commanding investigation into these matters. The Quran elsewhere commands us فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ بَدَأَ ٱلْخَلْقَ - “Look carefully at how creation began.” Allah ﷻ wants us to study creation and its origins. Muslims shouldn’t be afraid of these studies - we should engage in them. The origin of species, the development of life, the structure of the universe - these are subjects Allah ﷻ invites us to explore.

Our intellectual tradition needs to produce the highest levels of academic material on these subjects, which can then be adapted into accessible literature for the general population. You always have two kinds of materials: academic papers for scholars and accessible literature for everyone else. The vast majority of people need the accessible version, but that accessible material must be based on solid academic research.

The disbelievers of the Prophet’s time couldn’t have directly observed that the heavens and earth were once fused - they could only think about how everything was nothing before Allah ﷻ created it and separated it. But now we live in an age where we can literally attempt to peer into the origins of the universe. When you study ayahs like these, watching documentaries about the origin of the universe can actually deepen your understanding and increase your iman.

What’s truly sad is that many brilliant people study the universe and become utterly fascinated by this incredible creation that seems to go on infinitely - yet all their amazement stops at the universe itself. They’re impressed with creation but not with the Creator. When a believer sees the same wonders, they become even more impressed with Allah ﷻ, the One who designed and created all of this.

The ayah ends with a question: أَفَلَا يُؤْمِنُونَ - then why don’t they believe? With all this evidence before them, what prevents them from faith?

Ayah 31

وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًا سُبُلًا لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ

And We placed on the earth firmly set mountains, lest it should shift with them, and We made therein [mountain] passes [as] roads that they might be guided.

After discussing the origins of the universe and life, the ayah turns to the earth’s features. وَجَعَلْنَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ رَوَٰسِىَ - We placed on the earth tall mountains.

The word رَوَاسِي (plural of راسِيَة) is fascinating. It comes from the verb رَسَا, which means to anchor a ship. The ports where large ships drop their anchors are called مَرْسَى for this reason. Allah ﷻ compares mountains to ships whose anchors have been laid down. This imagery is profound because one day, those anchors will be lifted. When you lift a ship’s anchor, what happens? The ship sails away. Allah ﷻ describes the Day of Judgment using this same imagery - the mountains will literally be sailing through the sky.

The term رَوَاسِي also refers to mountain ranges - one mountain feeding into another, creating vast connected systems.

أَن تَمِيدَ بِهِمْ - so that it doesn’t shift with them. The verb مَادَ means to rattle and then tip over. If these massive mountains weren’t properly anchored, there’s a possibility they could collapse due to winds, earthquakes, or other forces. But Allah ﷻ says He anchored them so well that they don’t tip over.

وَجَعَلْنَا فِيهَا فِجَاجًا سُبُلًا - and We placed in those mountain ranges vast valleys and convenient roadways. When children draw mountains, they typically draw two peaks with a tiny path between them, barely wide enough to walk. But actual valleys between mountains can be incredibly vast - entire cities can exist in the plains between mountain ranges. فِجَاج refers to these vast, open valleys. سُبُل means convenient, well-traveled roads.

Allah ﷻ naturally made these pathways for humanity. Without them, traveling through mountainous regions would be nearly impossible. Once you go up into a mountain range, everything looks the same - you can easily lose your way. But when there’s a clear pathway, you can follow it with confidence that you’re heading in the right direction.

لَّعَلَّهُمْ يَهْتَدُونَ - so that they may find their way, so that they may be committed to guidance. On one level, this refers to physical guidance - the mountains and pathways help people navigate. But the deeper meaning is spiritual: We made such a beautiful, functional highway system for you, enabling your civilizations to develop and flourish. That should be enough for you to be grateful and come to guidance toward Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 32

وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ سَقْفًا مَّحْفُوظًا ۖ وَهُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهَا مُعْرِضُونَ

And We made the sky a protected ceiling, but they, from its signs, are turning away.

This is the ayah referenced earlier about the believer’s perspective on the universe. وَجَعَلْنَا ٱلسَّمَآءَ سَقْفًا - We made the sky as a ceiling. The word سَقْف specifically means the ceiling as seen from inside, not the roof viewed from outside. So whatever you can see with your most powerful telescopes is still the inside of the ceiling. There’s an outside to it that remains beyond our reach.

مَّحْفُوظًا - and it’s guarded, protected. The sky itself has a protection system built into it. We can see easy manifestations of this protection. When a large meteor enters the earth’s atmosphere, what happens? It begins to disintegrate due to atmospheric friction. By the time it reaches the ground, what was once massive has been reduced to a small rock or even just dust. The sky itself guards the earth.

Yet despite all these signs, وَهُمْ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِهَا مُعْرِضُونَ - they turn away from its ayat, its signs. The same people who might study the universe professionally, who might marvel at its wonders, still turn away from recognizing these phenomena as signs of Allah ﷻ. They see the mechanics but miss the Designer.

Ayah 33

وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ ۖ كُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ

And it is He who created the night and the day and the sun and the moon; all [heavenly bodies] in an orbit are swimming.

The final ayah in this sequence describes the celestial order. وَهُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَ ٱلَّيْلَ وَٱلنَّهَارَ - and He is the One who created the night and the day, وَٱلشَّمْسَ وَٱلْقَمَرَ - and the sun and the moon.

Notice an interesting subtlety in the sequence. Allah ﷻ mentions night first, then day. Then He mentions the sun first, then the moon. You might expect Him to mention the moon first since He just mentioned night, and the moon is associated with night. But He reverses the order.

This reversal follows a deeper principle in Arabic rhetoric. You don’t always go by the surface sequence - sometimes you go by the fundamental sequence. What’s the original, default state? The original state is darkness. Darkness is the normal condition, and then light disturbs that darkness. Similarly, between the sun and moon, which is the original light source? The sun is the default, primary light. The moon borrows and reflects light. So the originals in both pairs are mentioned first: night (original state) and day (secondary state), sun (original light) and moon (borrowed light).

كُلٌّ فِى فَلَكٍ يَسْبَحُونَ - all of them are in their own orbits, swimming. The word فَلَك means orbit - a rounded path. يَسْبَحُونَ comes from سَبَحَ, meaning to swim. But فَلَك also implies something circular, a ship that makes a complete journey and returns to where it started. This is the perfect description of an orbit.

Even centuries ago, Muslims were discussing how the sun and moon move in their own orbits. The Quran presented this reality long before modern astronomy confirmed these orbital patterns, using the beautiful imagery of celestial bodies swimming through space in their appointed paths.

Ayah 34–46 · linked video: 03. Al-Anbya (Ayah 34-46)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 34-46

Ayah 34

وَمَا جَعَلْنَا لِبَشَرٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ ٱلْخُلْدَ ۖ أَفَإِي۟ن مِّتَّ فَهُمُ ٱلْخَـٰلِدُونَ

Some disbelievers in Makkah would comfort themselves by saying, “Don’t worry about Muhammad ﷺ. He’s going to die eventually, and his message will die with him.” Allah ﷻ responds directly to this foolish notion: “We never gave any human being before you eternal life.” If the greatest prophets and messengers who came before didn’t receive immortality, what makes them think the Prophet ﷺ would be any different?

The ayah continues with a powerful rhetorical question: “So is it the case that if you die, they’re going to live forever?” The disbelievers speak so confidently about the Prophet’s ﷺ death, as if they themselves have been granted eternal life. This is the height of delusion—to acknowledge that someone else will die while somehow imagining that you yourself won’t face the same fate.

Ayah 35

كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ذَآئِقَةُ ٱلْمَوْتِ ۗ وَنَبْلُوكُم بِٱلشَّرِّ وَٱلْخَيْرِ فِتْنَةً ۖ وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ

Every single person is going to taste death. Notice the word “taste”—death is something you will experience intimately. It’s not something abstract that happens to other people. You will know it personally.

But this life isn’t just about waiting for death. Allah ﷻ tells us: “We will test you with evil and with good.” The word الشَّرّ refers to the difficulties, the hardships, the things we perceive as bad for us. But we’re also tested with الْخَيْر —the good times, the blessings, the prosperity. Both are described as فِتْنَة, a difficult test.

The word fitnah is profound here. It comes from the process of purifying gold, where the metal is melted under intense heat to separate the pure from the impure. You’re going to be tested very intensely. You’re going to be put under the flame. The good times test whether you’ll be grateful or arrogant. The difficult times test whether you’ll be patient or despairing.

And then the ayah ends with certainty: وَإِلَيْنَا تُرْجَعُونَ—“And to Us alone you will be returned.” Not to your wealth, not to your status, not to your tribe. To Allah ﷻ alone.

Ayah 36

وَإِذَا رَءَاكَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ إِن يَتَّخِذُونَكَ إِلَّا هُزُوًا أَهَـٰذَا ٱلَّذِى يَذْكُرُ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ وَهُم بِذِكْرِ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ هُمْ كَـٰفِرُونَ

When the disbelievers saw the Prophet ﷺ, they took him for nothing but a joke. They didn’t take him seriously at all. They would say to each other mockingly, “This is the guy who’s talking about your gods? Really? Him?” They spoke about him with complete disdain—who does he think he is to challenge what we’ve been worshiping?

But notice the striking contrast Allah ﷻ presents: while they mock the Prophet ﷺ for mentioning their false gods, they themselves are in complete denial when it comes to the remembrance of الرَّحْمَـٰن, the Most Merciful. They reject the One who deserves all remembrance and worship, yet they’re offended when someone points out the falsehood of stones and statues. This is the backwards nature of disbelief—defending what has no power while rejecting the All-Powerful.

Ayah 37

خُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ ۚ سَأُو۟رِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِى فَلَا تَسْتَعْجِلُونِ

The human being was created with عَجَل (hastiness) as one of his ingredients. Just as we’re made of bones, flesh and skin, we’re also made of rushing. It’s part of our very being—we want everything quickly. This applies to good things and bad things alike. Even the Prophet ﷺ had this quality, but in the most beautiful way. Some scholars mention that he would rush to meet Allah ﷻ through his eagerness for prayer.

This life is designed around instant gratification. Shaytan’s temptations are so powerful because he says, “I’ll give you pleasure right now. I’ll fulfill your desire immediately.” And Allah ﷻ says, “Be patient. I’ll give it to you in Jannah.” But we want it now. You can see this even in children—tell them they can have something in ten minutes and watch them struggle. “Are we there yet? Can we go home? Why do we have to be here?” That’s when you recite: خُلِقَ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِنْ عَجَلٍ.

The disbelievers were demanding, “Bring the punishment! If you’re really a messenger, let’s see some action from the Creator of the heavens and earth!” Allah ﷻ responds: “I will show you My signs, so don’t rush Me. Don’t rush.” Everything will come in its appointed time.

Ayah 38

وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

They keep saying, “When is this promise going to be fulfilled, if you’re really telling the truth?” They demand to know when the Day of Judgment will come, when they’ll face consequences. This mockery reveals their fundamental disbelief—they don’t actually think there will be any reckoning.

Ayah 39

لَوْ يَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ حِينَ لَا يَكُفُّونَ عَن وُجُوهِهِمُ ٱلنَّارَ وَلَا عَن ظُهُورِهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يُنصَرُونَ

If only the disbelievers knew about that day when they won’t be able to raise their palms to protect their faces from the Fire. The word كَفّ means to raise your palm in defense. When something comes at you—especially at your face—your first instinct is to throw your hand up to protect yourself. This is pure reflex. Even a baby’s hand accidentally coming toward your face makes you react instantly.

But on that Day, their hands will be held back. They won’t even be able to shield their faces from the flames. And it’s not just their faces—their backs will be burning too, and they won’t be able to defend against that either. And no one will come to help them. They’ll be completely abandoned to the punishment they denied.

Ayah 40

بَلْ تَأْتِيهِم بَغْتَةً فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ فَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ رَدَّهَا وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ

The reality is that the punishment will come at them suddenly, out of nowhere. بَغْتَة means something appears that you couldn’t have even imagined was possible. It wasn’t on your radar at all, and suddenly it’s right there.

فَتَبْهَتُهُمْ—it will shock them into complete silence. Have you ever been so shocked that you can’t even speak? That’s بَهْت. They’ll be frozen, unable to respond, unable to process what’s happening.

Then they won’t be able to cancel it or push it away. And here’s something deeply painful: وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ—“Nor will they even be looked at.” They won’t even receive a glance of mercy. In this world, when people want to generate compassion, they show images of suffering. Humanitarian organizations display pictures of those in pain, and people’s hearts move. “Look at what they’re going through. Look at their condition.” The idea is that if you just looked, you would feel moved to help.

But on that Day, Allah ﷻ won’t even look at them. They’ll cry and beg for mercy—“At least look at us! See what we’re going through!”—but there will be no response.

Ayah 41

وَلَقَدِ ٱسْتُهْزِئَ بِرُسُلٍ مِّن قَبْلِكَ فَحَاقَ بِٱلَّذِينَ سَخِرُوا۟ مِنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ

Why does Allah ﷻ mention this ayah right here? Because the previous passages described the terrible fate of those who mocked the Prophet ﷺ. So Allah ﷻ consoles His Messenger: “Don’t worry about their mockery. Messengers before you were also ridiculed.”

The word اسْتُهْزِئَ refers to mockery done behind someone’s back, in private circles where people pretend to go along but then ridicule when they’re among themselves. سَخِرُوا is public humiliation—putting someone down openly, trying to embarrass them in front of others.

But here’s what happened: whatever they used to mock fell upon them and surrounded them completely. The word حَاقَ describes something dropping from the sky like a cage, falling and trapping what’s inside. Imagine an animal trap that falls and encloses completely—there’s no escape. The very thing they laughed about became their prison, surrounding them entirely. The punishment for their mockery encased them with no way out.

Ayah 42

قُلْ مَن يَكْلَؤُكُم بِٱلَّيْلِ وَٱلنَّهَارِ مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ ۗ بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ

Allah ﷻ tells the Prophet ﷺ to ask them: “Who keeps constant watch over you during the night and day?” The word يَكْلَأ means to protect someone continuously, vigilantly, like a security detail that never sleeps. If a security guard falls asleep even for a moment when someone is a target, that person is finished.

Who protects you with that level of constant vigilance? مِنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰن—from Ar-Rahman, the Most Merciful. And here’s the profound implication: He protects you from Himself, from His own anger and power. If Allah ﷻ didn’t protect you constantly, you would be destroyed in an instant.

But despite this continuous protection and mercy, بَلْ هُمْ عَن ذِكْرِ رَبِّهِم مُّعْرِضُونَ—they completely ignore the remembrance of their Master. They turn away from the very One who keeps them alive every single moment.

Ayah 43

أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَالِهَةٌ تَمْنَعُهُم مِّن دُونِنَا ۚ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَ أَنفُسِهِمْ وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ

Or do they have other gods besides Us that can protect them? Those gods they’re so devoted to—can they even help themselves? No. They can’t even aid their own selves, let alone protect their worshipers.

وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ—nor will those gods give them company against Us. To understand this phrase, imagine the old Bedouin custom: if someone committed a crime in a village and then needed to pass through that territory again, he would be in danger. So he would ask a powerful member of that tribe to accompany him for protection. That person’s presence would keep him safe as he passed through.

These people think that on the Day of Judgment, when they’re in trouble with Allah ﷻ, they’ll just bring their idols along to protect them. But those idols won’t be there. They won’t provide any company or protection whatsoever.

Ayah 44

بَلْ مَتَّعْنَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ وَءَابَآءَهُمْ حَتَّىٰ طَالَ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلْعُمُرُ ۗ أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ أَنَّا نَأْتِى ٱلْأَرْضَ نَنقُصُهَا مِنْ أَطْرَافِهَآ ۚ أَفَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ

The reality is that We gave these people and their ancestors comfort and provision. We gave the people of Makkah everything—status, power, respect throughout Arabia, water in a place where nothing grows. They enjoyed this privileged position for so long that they became complacent. They thought it would last forever.

But don’t they see that We are coming to the earth, reducing it from all sides? Imagine this as a map of tribal territories. Originally, the disbelieving tribes controlled vast areas—let’s say it’s all one color on a map. The Muslim influence was tiny, just Madinah and a few scattered believers. But after events like the Treaty of Hudaybiyyah, the sphere of influence began to shift. The Prophet ﷺ made agreements with tribes, and the map started changing. The area controlled by the disbelievers began shrinking while the reach of Islam expanded.

The people of Makkah couldn’t even flee south to Yemen for support, because Yemen was Christian territory, and just a year before the Prophet’s ﷺ birth, an army from Yemen had tried to destroy Makkah. Their options were running out. Their influence was deteriorating.

أَفَلَا يَرَوْنَ—don’t they see the signs already? This is being revealed in Makkah, before the Muslims even realize how dramatically the situation will change. And then Allah ﷻ asks: أَفَهُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ—or are they really the dominant ones, as they imagine themselves to be?

Ayah 45

قُلْ إِنَّمَآ أُنذِرُكُم بِٱلْوَحْىِ ۚ وَلَا يَسْمَعُ ٱلصُّمُّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا مَا يُنذَرُونَ

Tell them: “I’m only warning you by means of revelation.” The Prophet ﷺ is clarifying his position. He’s not here to argue back and forth or engage in trash talk. This isn’t his word—it’s Allah’s ﷻ Word.

The disbelievers might be thinking, “This orphan with his handful of followers is threatening us? He’s saying our land will shrink and we’ll be defeated? Who does he think he is?” The Prophet ﷺ responds: I’m conveying revelation. These aren’t my words. Allah ﷻ speaks as the Master to His slaves, and He doesn’t hold back. I have no authority to change what Allah ﷻ has said. If you’re offended, be offended by Allah ﷻ, not by me.

And then comes an even more direct statement: “The deaf don’t hear the call, no matter how much they’re warned.” They’ve closed their ears and hearts to the truth. No amount of warning makes a difference to them.

Ayah 46

وَلَئِن مَّسَّتْهُمْ نَفْحَةٌ مِّنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ لَيَقُولُنَّ يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ

This final ayah is remarkable in how it illustrates the reality of Allah’s ﷻ punishment through layers of linguistic precision. Let’s examine it carefully.

The ayah begins with لَئِن—“even if.” In Arabic, when you start with “if,” it indicates something unlikely. Compare this to “when”—if I say “when I go to campus,” I’ve expressed clear intention. But “if I go to campus” suggests maybe I won’t. So already there’s a degree of weakness in the likelihood.

Then we have the word مَّسَّتْهُمْ—“it touched them.” مَسّ is the lightest form of contact, like barely grazing something. In Surah Yasin, when the people say لَا تَمَسُّونِ (don’t touch me), they mean don’t even come near me, don’t make the slightest contact.

So we have: “Even if—unlikely as it is—it barely touched them…”

Touched them with what? نَفْحَة—a draft of air. Specifically, نَفْحَة (with the letter noon) is a cool breeze that wisps through when you close a door or window. It’s the gentlest, most subtle form of air movement. If it were hot air (with the letter lam, it would be لَفْحَة), but this is cool air, even less noticeable.

Now understand the scene: they’re not even in Hell yet. The door to Hell is closing, and a tiny bit of air escapes out. That air, coming from the raging Fire, is somehow cool by the time it reaches them.

And this isn’t just any punishment—it’s مِنْ عَذَابِ رَبِّكَ—“from the punishment of your Master.” Allah ﷻ could have said “from their punishment” or “from Allah’s punishment” or “from the Mighty’s punishment”—any of these would emphasize the severity. But He says “your Master,” addressing the Prophet ﷺ. When Allah ﷻ mentions His Messenger, the ayah is filled with mercy. So He makes this already-weakened punishment even weaker by adding that element of mercy.

Let’s review what we have: an unlikely scenario, of barely touching, with the coolest form of contact (air), from a merciful source. Of all the things that can touch you—solid, liquid, gas—air is what you feel the least. And if it’s cool air, you barely notice it at all.

Yet if even this—this impossibly light, cool draft of air from the punishment of your merciful Master—were to touch them, لَيَقُولُنَّ—they will definitely, absolutely say: يَـٰوَيْلَنَآ—“What destruction has befallen us!”

The word وَيْل in Arabic describes the worst possible thing that could happen. It’s used in religious texts to refer to a valley in Hell that Hell itself is afraid of. This isn’t some upper level of punishment—this is the deepest dungeon of the Fire.

And this person hasn’t even entered Hell. He’s outside, experiencing the faintest cool breeze, and he’s screaming, “Wayl! How could anything be worse than this?!”

Then comes their confession: إِنَّا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ—“We have always been wrongdoers.” When a criminal is arrested, his first response is: “I didn’t do it. It wasn’t me. Where’s my lawyer?” Then he tries to come up with an alibi, excuses, anything. But when a criminal is pushed beyond his limits, tortured past the point of resistance, he finally admits: “Yes, I did it. I’ve always been wrong. I was never right.”

That’s the confession this person makes after experiencing nothing more than a cool draft of air. And remember—his lawyers are gone (وَلَا هُم مِّنَّا يُصْحَبُونَ from ayah 43). There’s no one to defend him, no excuses left, nothing but the admission of guilt: “We have always been criminals.”

May Allah ﷻ protect us from even a whisper of the punishment of the Fire, and may He grant us understanding of these ayahs so we can live by them.

Ayah 47–52 · linked video: 04. Al-Anbya (Ayah 47-52)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 47-52

Ayah 47

وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَـٰسِبِينَ

And We place the scales of justice for the Day of Resurrection, so no soul will be treated unjustly at all. And if there is [even] the weight of a mustard seed, We will bring it forth. And sufficient are We as accountant.

Right after Allah ﷻ mentions punishment in the previous ayah, He establishes how someone ends up facing that punishment. The ayah begins: وَنَضَعُ ٱلْمَوَٰزِينَ ٱلْقِسْطَ - “We place the truly just scales.” The word الْقِسْطَ is particularly powerful here. In Arabic grammar, this is a masdar (infinitive/verbal noun) being used as an adjective. The beauty of this construction is that when you describe something with the concept itself rather than just an adjective, you’re saying you will never find more justice than these scales. They don’t merely possess fairness - they are fairness itself, justice itself.

The ayah continues: لِيَوْمِ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ - “for the Day of Resurrection,” فَلَا تُظْلَمُ نَفْسٌ شَيْـًٔا - “and no soul will be wronged in anything.” The comprehensiveness is absolute - no individual will be harmed, mistreated, or dealt with unjustly in any way, shape or form.

Then comes the remarkable precision: وَإِن كَانَ مِثْقَالَ حَبَّةٍ مِّنْ خَرْدَلٍ أَتَيْنَا بِهَا - “And even if there was the weight of a mustard seed, We would bring it forward.” The evidence will emerge no matter how small. The ayah concludes: وَكَفَىٰ بِنَا حَـٰسِبِينَ - “And We by Ourselves are enough to take account.”

This raises an important question: if Allah ﷻ has complete knowledge and is ultimately fair, why have the Day of Judgment at all? Why not send people straight to their destination? The answer reveals something profound about divine justice.

Justice doesn’t just need to be executed - it needs to be perceived as having been executed. There’s a fundamental difference between the execution of justice and the perception of justice. When a judge makes a fair decision in secret, people may doubt its fairness simply because it was hidden. Secret trials are almost universally perceived as unfair, regardless of their actual merit.

If Judgment Day occurred in secret, people might argue: “I don’t know how He judged me. He put me in Hell, but I thought I did pretty good.” But when judgment unfolds with complete transparency - when you see your own record, when your own limbs testify, when every deed is accounted for - no excuse remains. It’s like a teacher who shows you your graded exam. You might dispute a grade you only hear about, but when you see your marked paper and recognize your own mistakes, you’re satisfied with the result. You cannot complain anymore.

The scales being established with ultimate fairness, and the entire proceeding of Judgment Day, exist so we have no room for excuses. This matters especially for disbelievers who never had trust in Allah ﷻ during their worldly life. They need to see: this is exactly what I earned, and this is exactly what I got.

Ayah 48

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ وَضِيَآءً وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ

And We had certainly given Musa and Harun the criterion and a light and a reminder for the righteous.

We return here to the normal sequence in the Quran - Musa (AS) mentioned before Harun (AS). The previous surah contained a special story about Harun (AS) with unique features, where the sequence was reversed. Now we’re back to the typical pattern.

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ - “And We gave Musa and Harun Al-Furqan.” The word الْفُرْقَانَ comes from the root فرق. meaning to separate. The form فُرْقَان intensifies this meaning - it’s the powerful separator, the one who clearly distinguishes right from wrong. We gave Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) revelation that made it abundantly clear what right is and what wrong is.

This mention is especially significant after the accounts in the previous surah. When people fell into shirk, it wasn’t because they lacked clarity about what tawheed is or isn’t. They had the Furqan - the clear criterion - yet they abandoned what they knew.

وَضِيَآءً - “And diya.” Arabic has two words for light: نور and ضياء. Diya refers to warm light with heat, like sunlight. Nur can be cooler, like moonlight. The choice of diya here suggests something about the nature of the Sharia given to Musa (AS) and Harun (AS) - it was tougher, more demanding than what was given to us. Some provisions in their law were relaxed in Islam. Things that were harder on them became easier for us. Allah ﷻ describes this in another ayah as removing “the shackles and chains that used to be on them.”

They had to worship the entire day of Saturday with no business permitted. We have Friday prayer, then return to work and enjoy ourselves. Allah ﷻ says: فَإِذَا قُضِيَتِ الصَّلَاةُ فَانتَشِرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ - “And when the prayer has been concluded, disperse within the land” وَابْتَغُوا مِن فَضْلِ اللَّهِ - “and seek from the bounty of Allah” (62:10). Get out, go have fun, pursue Allah’s favor. That’s from Allah’s ease to us, among many other relaxations.

وَذِكْرًا لِّلْمُتَّقِينَ - “And a reminder for those who have taqwa.” This is crucial. People often think of religious revelation - the Torah, the Quran - only as law, as instructions: do this, don’t do that. But Allah ﷻ says it’s not just laws and instructions; it’s a reminder.

A reminder is something you engage with regularly. Consider the alarm on your phone - it’s not a one-time thing, it goes off every day, constantly prompting you. Law you can simply know. You know to stop at a red light without studying the traffic manual daily. You studied once for the test you barely remember taking, and that’s it. But a reminder is something you need all the time. You need to be reminded to wake up, so you set an alarm. You need reminders of your appointments, so you mark your calendar with alerts.

Revelation is supposed to be a distinguishing between right and wrong, clear as day so you can walk your path with certainty. It’s also supposed to be a constant reminder for people who have taqwa. There’s a beautiful cycle here: people who receive reminder from revelation constantly reinforce their taqwa, and they develop that taqwa because they’re being constantly reminded through revelation.

Ayah 49

ٱلَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ

Who fear their Lord in the unseen, while they are of the Hour apprehensive.

الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِٱلْغَيْبِ - “Those who truly fear their Master in the unseen.” The word يَخْشَوْنَ carries the meaning of being overwhelmed with fear, not just casual concern. This describes their relationship with their Master who is in the unseen - their Rabb whom they cannot see.

The phrase بِٱلْغَيْبِ has another dimension: they fear Allah ﷻ in the unseen from others. They don’t just show fear of Allah ﷻ in public; they fear Him when nobody else is watching. Their consciousness of Allah ﷻ doesn’t depend on human observers.

وَهُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّاعَةِ مُشْفِقُونَ - “And they, from the Hour, are apprehensive.” When it comes to the Hour specifically, they are terrified, overwhelmed with fear. The word مُشْفِقُونَ is fascinating - شَفِيق means someone extremely merciful who removes fears from others. But مُشْفِق is someone filled with fear themselves. Arabic sometimes uses words that connect to their opposites in meaning.

Ayah 50

وَهَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ ۚ أَفَأَنتُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ

And this [Quran] is a blessed message which We have sent down. Then are you with it deniers?

وَهَـٰذَا ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ - “And this is a blessed reminder.” This is a description of the Quran itself - a reminder that is مُبَارَك (full of barakah). You’re not just receiving a reminder; the very act of engaging with the Quran brings barakah. The words coming out of your mouth - each letter earns you ten good deeds. The environment you’re in becomes surrounded by angels. Goodness flows from this gathering far beyond just listening to words.

أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ - “We have sent it down,” فَأَنتُمْ لَهُۥ مُنكِرُونَ - “Then are you going to deny it? Are you going to reject it altogether?”

What we learn from this is profound: the recitation of Quran and remembering Allah ﷻ through the Quran brings about good things in life that you didn’t even expect. Barakah is contagious. It’s goodness that exceeds expectation. When you have more good than what’s normally expected, that’s called barakah.

You’re sitting at home reciting Quran, and suddenly the interview goes through. That proposal you submitted gets approved - or better yet, gets rejected because you discover the arrangement would have been harmful. Something good comes your way because of the barakah of the Quran. Simply having a Quran lifestyle brings abundant good into your life, beyond even the spiritual rewards of the remembrance itself.

Ayah 51

۞ وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ رُشْدَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ وَكُنَّا بِهِۦ عَـٰلِمِينَ

And We had certainly given Ibrahim his sound judgment before, and We were of him well-knowing.

وَلَقَدْ ءَاتَيْنَآ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ رُشْدَهُۥ مِن قَبْلُ - “We had already given Ibrahim his rushd way back.” رُشْد means upright character, sound judgment, a sharp and righteous mind. This was given to him even before revelation, before all of that. Allah ﷻ had granted him an upright, discerning nature.

وَكُنَّا بِهِۦ عَـٰلِمِينَ - “And We had full knowledge of what he was up to. We had full knowledge of what he was doing.”

This statement is critically important to mention before we enter the story of Ibrahim (AS), because he did things - many things - that we are not allowed to do. He attempted to slaughter his child. That’s illegal in Islam. He left his family in the middle of what would otherwise be certain death. You cannot do that to your family. You can’t say, “I’m following the Sunnah of Ibrahim,” take your family to the Mojave Desert and drive away. That’s not how this works.

He broke idols, which means you cannot sneak into a temple at night and destroy religious artifacts. This actually happened. I met a brother in Duluth, Minnesota - beautiful small city by the water with a small but vibrant Muslim community of about twelve people for jumu’ah. This Malaysian brother told me about an extreme cultist movement back in Malaysia. A speaker gave talks saying, “We have to establish the Sunnah of Ibrahim (AS). There are many Buddhists and Hindus in Malaysia, and though we’re the majority Muslim population, we must break the idols. That’s how we’ll teach the people.”

Young teenagers, lacking wisdom but full of zeal, started sneaking into Hindu and Buddhist temples at night, destroying idols. It was completely haram, but they were doing it based on a misunderstanding of the story of Ibrahim (AS). What does a teenager need except an ayah taken out of context? Suddenly he thinks he’s qualified to issue religious rulings for his entire country, and he takes action with devastating consequences.

From what this brother told me, in the 1970s or 1980s, the Hindu community discovered what was happening. Extremists among them laid an ambush, waiting in hiding. When these Muslim kids showed up to climb the temple, they were brutally killed. Muslim teenagers died, riots erupted in the streets, chaos spread, and Islam was blamed. Ibrahim (AS) was blamed.

I once heard a comedian on NPR - a female comic - talking about Ibrahim (AS). She said, “I was trying to read the Bible and figure it out for myself, but I’m really confused. Why would God make anybody kill their son? Anybody who does that in our society is a psychopathic criminal. Why do we idolize this man? And why is he jumping into fire - isn’t that suicide?”

If you look at the things Ibrahim (AS) was commanded to do, we genuinely cannot do any of them. They’re very difficult to justify through conventional logic alone. What kind of religion is this? But you must understand: the instructions and tests given to prophets are far harder and far more difficult than the tests given to regular believers.

His tests were only legitimate because they were directly revealed to him by Allah ﷻ. Second, when revelation applies to someone other than a prophet, and the prophet goes out of his way to preach that message, that’s when it becomes applicable to others. A prophet never keeps a message to himself unless it’s exclusively for him.

Our Prophet ﷺ was given certain instructions that were only for him. Tahajjud prayer was mandatory for him, yet he never commanded us or obligated us to pray tahajjud. In fact, he went out of his way to ensure that some of his practices exclusive to him didn’t become mandatory parts of the religion, because that would make life unbearably hard on us.

Ibrahim (AS) never went around preaching, “Kill your sons for reward” or anything of that nature. These were specific instructions to him. Moreover, nobody would do these things on their own initiative. Ibrahim (AS) didn’t just wake up one day and decide to destroy idols. Before Allah ﷻ mentions the story of him destroying the idols, we learn something crucial: this wasn’t an idea he had himself. This was something Allah ﷻ inspired him to do for that particular community. This was something Allah ﷻ instructed him to do.

Ayah 52

إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَقَوْمِهِۦ مَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلتَّمَاثِيلُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَـٰكِفُونَ

When he said to his father and his people, “What are these statues to which you are devoted?”

إِذْ قَالَ لِأَبِيهِ - “When he said to his father.” Some scholars say this refers to his uncle, because أب can be a comprehensive term used for any elder in the family. They argue his biological father couldn’t have been a mushrik. Both views exist in our tradition.

وَقَوْمِهِۦ - “And his people,” his entire nation.

مَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلتَّمَاثِيلُ - “What are these tamaatheel?” He calls them تَمَاثِيل - representations. This word is significant. Even the people who commit shirk recognize that what they’re looking at isn’t itself what they worship. It represents something greater. The idol itself isn’t considered sacred by the philosophically minded among them; what it represents is sacred.

This is what the learned teachers in idol-worshipping traditions will tell you even today. The philosophers of Hinduism will explain: “We don’t worship the idols at all. That’s just stone. We worship the ideas, the divine concepts behind them. The physical representations have no intrinsic value.”

But what happens to the masses of people? The common people don’t follow this philosophical understanding of Hinduism - which at its highest levels speaks of God having no image, being beyond representation, with no picture able to capture Him, and there being only One God. This exists in sophisticated Hindu philosophy, but does it make its way down to regular people? No.

When you have something that can be misunderstood by a child, it will be misunderstood by society. That’s a simple formula, especially for religious practices. If a practice or symbol can be misinterpreted at a basic level, it will cause confusion at a societal level.

Consider poetry written in praise of the Prophet ﷺ. Writing such poetry is completely fine. But imagine a poet depicting himself on the Day of Judgment, writing as though he’s there, saying to the Prophet ﷺ: “Who is going to help us if not you? Everyone else has said no. You’re the only one who can say yes.” The poet is imagining the scene, and this is what poets do - they create vivid imagery.

But when thousands of readers encounter this poetry, do you think everyone will understand the artistic context? Or will they read it and internalize it differently? When thousands read “Who will help me except you?” directed at the Prophet ﷺ, does that become a theological problem? Absolutely.

I’m not saying that author is wrong or the poetry is inherently problematic. The poetry may be fine in its proper place and context. But if the community - the general population - isn’t mature enough to understand where this is coming from and what it truly means, then it shouldn’t be popularized in that form.

The things that should be popularized are what Allah ﷻ already commanded us to popularize. The Prophet ﷺ told us: بَلِّغُوا عَنِّي وَلَوْ آيَةً - “Convey from me even if it’s one ayah.” Even one ayah can potentially be taken the wrong way, but Allah ﷻ protects you from these dangers when you follow what He commanded.

You won’t find better praise of Allah ﷻ, better praise of Muhammad Rasulullah ﷺ than what exists in the Quran itself. Other expressions will have their place, but these alternative forms are slowly becoming substitutes for the Quran. They’re becoming replacements for the actual praise of Allah ﷻ that He revealed.

I’m not going to give a fatwa about nasheeds or make them haram - I don’t know. But I feel like they’re replacing the Quran. That same love and affection you’re supposed to have for Allah’s Book is being redirected. Then people say, “Well, I don’t understand the Quran. It doesn’t do anything for me.”

Is that Allah’s problem or yours? Should you seek a substitute rather than work on yourself? Do you really underestimate the Quran so much that you think it won’t satisfy your spiritual needs? You’re short-changing what Allah ﷻ called ذِكْرٌ مُّبَارَكٌ - a blessed reminder.

This isn’t just any book. This is the greatest message ever given. This is the Book of Allah ﷻ, the Word revealed to Muhammad ﷺ. It’s not a small thing. We shouldn’t underestimate its value in our own personal spiritual development. We shouldn’t take it for granted.

Returning to the ayah: Ibrahim (AS) says to his father and his nation, مَا هَـٰذِهِ ٱلتَّمَاثِيلُ ٱلَّتِىٓ أَنتُمْ لَهَا عَـٰكِفُونَ - “What are these representations that you sit in devotion before?” He calls them تَمَاثِيل - representations - because even they recognize this isn’t what they worship. This represents something greater, they claim.

Ibrahim (AS) made this public. He first spoke to his father about it privately, but his father wouldn’t listen. So he went out into the community and started asking directly: “What do you worship?”

“This represents the rain.”

“How does he represent the rain? How does he do that?”

“This is the god of wealth.”

“How is he the god of wealth? You just made him from wood yesterday. You lost money making him. How is it the god of wealth?”

Yet you’re sitting there - عَـٰكِفُونَ - in complete concentration, in devoted attention before it.

Ayah 53–68 · linked video: 05. Al-Anbya (Ayah 53-68)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 53-68

Ayah 53

قَالُوا۟ وَجَدْنَآ ءَابَآءَنَا لَهَا عَـٰبِدِينَ

After Ibrahim (AS) questioned his people about their worship of idols, they offered their final defense: “We found our ancestors worshiping these things in this way.” This response reveals one of the most persistent logical fallacies in human discourse - appealing to tradition as proof of truth. They essentially told young Ibrahim (AS) to watch himself and show respect for the way his father, grandfather and great-grandfather had always done things.

This appeal to tradition is a powerful tool for silencing truth. When someone speaks honestly about wrong practices, the response often comes: “That’s not how we do things. Show some respect for our tradition.” The person raising the concern is made to feel guilty for bringing it up at all.

We see this same pattern today in Muslim communities. Consider the young person who questions the Islamic validity of extravagant weddings - mixed gatherings, immodest display, questionable entertainment. When they raise these concerns, the response echoes the words of Ibrahim’s (AS) people: “Your uncle did it this way. Your cousin did it this way. That’s how our family does things. Don’t embarrass the family.”
