But we must ask: which tradition are we truly following? Look at the photographs of our great-grandparents - the men with their beards, the women with their hijab. Who has really abandoned the tradition? When Muslim weddings become indistinguishable from Hindu, Buddhist or Sikh ceremonies, we aren’t preserving our heritage. We’re returning to practices our ancestors left behind when they embraced Islam centuries ago.

This represents a new form of colonization - not political, but cultural. When we adopt shameless practices from cultures around us, we become a colonized people. There is nothing wrong with diverse cultures existing in the world, but when we embrace their immodesty and abandon our Islamic identity, we have lost something fundamental.

The erosion of hayah (shame, modesty, dignity) is particularly dangerous in our age. When filthy music videos play openly in restaurants claiming to serve halal food, when people can watch degrading content in front of their own family members without discomfort, something has died in the human conscience. What makes us human is our haya - Adam (AS) covered himself immediately when his clothing was removed, without any revelation commanding him to do so. Clothing itself is from Paradise, sent down as revelation. To lose this sense of shame is to lose our humanity.

Ayah 54

قَالَ لَقَدْ كُنتُمْ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُمْ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍ مُّبِينٍ

Ibrahim’s (AS) response was direct and unflinching. This hot-blooded young man declared: “You and your fathers have all been in clear error.” He didn’t soften his words or show deference to their beloved traditions. He identified their ancestral practices for exactly what they were - confusion and misguidance spanning generations.

This bold stance from a young man demonstrates an essential truth: when faced with falsehood, age and tradition cannot silence the voice of truth. Ibrahim (AS) understood that respect for elders and ancestors must be balanced with commitment to truth and tawhid (the oneness of Allah ﷻ).

Ayah 55

قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا بِٱلْحَقِّ أَمْ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱللَّـٰعِبِينَ

Their response reveals their disbelief that anyone, especially a youth, would seriously challenge their established religious system. “Are you for real?” they asked. “Or are you just playing around?” They assumed he must be jesting, because surely no one would genuinely attack an entire tradition this way. Kids say things when they’re young - surely he doesn’t really mean it.

This dismissal is another deflection tactic. Rather than addressing his arguments, they questioned whether he was even serious. It’s easier to write someone off as a joker than to confront uncomfortable truths.

Ayah 56

قَالَ بَل رَّبُّكُمْ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّذِى فَطَرَهُنَّ وَأَنَا۠ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

Ibrahim (AS) didn’t waver. “Rather, your Master is the Lord of the heavens and the earth - الَّذِى فَطَرَهُنَّ (the One who fashioned them all) - and I am a witness against all of you.” When they asked if he was bringing truth, he responded by pointing them to the ultimate Truth - Allah ﷻ, the Creator and Master of all existence.

The word فَطَرَهُنَّ emphasizes that Allah ﷻ is the One who originated and fashioned the heavens and earth. He is the Originator, not something that was itself created or fashioned by human hands. This directly contrasts with their idols, which they themselves carved and constructed.

Ibrahim (AS) positioned himself as a witness - شَاهِد - someone who stands by and testifies to the truth. He wasn’t backing down from his stance regardless of their reactions.

Ayah 57

وَتَٱللَّهِ لَأَكِيدَنَّ أَصْنَـٰمَكُم بَعْدَ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ

Now Ibrahim (AS) made an oath - and not just any oath, but one using the emphatic وَتَٱللَّهِ (wa tallahi - “And by Allah!”). The particle وَ before تَٱللَّهِ intensifies the oath. This is the kind of oath used when someone is genuinely angry, so upset that emotions are running high. It’s not the casual “wallahi” people sometimes use in everyday conversation, but the serious oath of someone making a solemn declaration.

“I will certainly scheme against your idols after you turn your backs,” he declared. This fifteen or sixteen-year-old young man was telling them directly: I’m going to do something to these idols of yours. Most adults probably dismissed it as the hot-blooded talk of youth. Kids say things. Who takes them seriously?

The timing he mentioned is significant - بَعْدَ أَن تُوَلُّوا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (after you turn away as departing ones). There was a major festival coming where everyone would gather in a large field for celebration. The entire town would empty out for this event. The idols would be left unattended.

An important clarification: some accounts mistakenly place these idols around the Kaaba, suggesting Ibrahim (AS) broke idols there. This doesn’t align with the historical sequence. Ibrahim (AS) was living in the area of Sham (the Levant) at this time. He made hijrah from there. Years later, when he had Ismail (AS), he traveled to the location of the Kaaba and raised its foundations with his son, as the Quran mentions: وَإِذْ يَرْفَعُ إِبْرَاهِيمُ الْقَوَاعِدَ مِنَ الْبَيْتِ (And when Ibrahim was raising the foundations of the House). This incident with the idols occurred in his homeland, not at the Kaaba.

Ayah 58

فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَٰذًا إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَّهُمْ لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ

Ibrahim (AS) made good on his oath. فَجَعَلَهُمْ جُذَٰذًا - he made them into judhadha, fragments. The word جُذَٰذ refers to the smallest fragments of something - tiny pieces, splinters, bits and pieces scattered everywhere. If you came home and saw a minuscule piece of plastic on the floor that was once your laptop, that would be judhādh.

When you break a large idol, it typically falls into a few large pieces - the head here, the torso there. But Ibrahim (AS) didn’t stop at breaking them. He pulverized them. He reduced them to such small fragments that you couldn’t even identify which pieces belonged to which idol. The face, the feet, the belly - everything was indistinguishable, crushed into countless tiny fragments scattered across the ground.

Why did he destroy them so thoroughly? Because if a recognizable face remained, they would simply attach it to a new body and continue worshiping. He left nothing they could salvage and reconstruct.

إِلَّا كَبِيرًا لَّهُمْ - except their large one. He left the biggest idol standing among all the rubble. لَعَلَّهُمْ إِلَيْهِ يَرْجِعُونَ - so that perhaps they would return to it, return to questioning it. The idea was that when they returned, they would see this lone idol standing among the destruction, and perhaps it would trigger some thought in their minds.

Ayah 59

قَالُوا۟ مَن فَعَلَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَآ إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

When the people returned from their festival and saw the scene, they were horrified. The place looked completely different - so much more open space now that the idols were gone. “Who did this to our gods?” they cried. إِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ - “Indeed, he is surely from the wrongdoers, from the oppressors!”

Imagine a Hindu temple where devotees climb thousands of steps up a mountain each morning to worship, only to find all their deities destroyed. The reaction would be explosive - riots, violence, homes burned, anyone who doesn’t belong to their faith targeted. This is the nature of people already misguided by shirk - when something triggers them, shaytan becomes extremely creative with them, leading them to all manner of crimes.

The town was in chaos, everyone trying to figure out who could have committed this act.

Ayah 60

قَالُوا۟ سَمِعْنَا فَتًى يَذْكُرُهُمْ يُقَالُ لَهُۥٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ

Some people spoke up: "We heard a فَتًى (a young man) talking about them, saying things against these idols. يُقَالُ لَهُۥٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمُ - We think he’s called Ibrahim.

Notice the uncertainty in their report. They heard a young man - they weren’t sure exactly who. Someone was saying things - they didn’t have direct quotes. They think his name was Ibrahim - even his identity wasn’t confirmed. It was all hearsay at this point. No one had solid evidence, just overheard comments and rumors. Someone had mentioned hearing a kid named Ibrahim speaking critically about the idols.

Ayah 61

قَالُوا۟ فَأْتُوا۟ بِهِۦ عَلَىٰ أَعْيُنِ ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ

The leaders ordered: “Bring him عَلَىٰ أَعْيُنِ ٱلنَّاسِ (before the eyes of the people) - in front of everyone.” This wasn’t going to be a private interrogation. They wanted a public trial, a public spectacle. لَعَلَّهُمْ يَشْهَدُونَ - “So that they may bear witness.”

Perhaps there were witnesses who saw him near the scene. Maybe someone could testify that they saw this young man acting suspiciously around the idols, giving them hostile looks, loitering near them. They wanted to gather any testimony from the crowd that might confirm his guilt.

Ayah 62

قَالُوٓا۟ ءَأَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَـٰذَا بِـَٔالِهَتِنَا يَـٰٓإِبْرَٰهِيمُ

When Ibrahim (AS) was brought before the assembly, they confronted him directly. ءَأَنتَ - “You!” The repetition of the word “you” in the Arabic shows emphasis and accusation. أَنتَ فَعَلْتَ هَـٰذَا - “Was it YOU who did this to our gods, O Ibrahim?”

They were trying to intimidate this young man by calling him out by name in front of hundreds of people, surrounded by the entire community demanding answers for the destruction of their beloved idols.

Ayah 63

قَالَ بَلْ فَعَلَهُۥ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَـٰذَا فَسْـَٔلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ يَنطِقُونَ

Ibrahim’s (AS) response demonstrated his unique style of argument and demonstration. بَلْ فَعَلَهُۥ كَبِيرُهُمْ هَـٰذَا - “Rather, this big one of theirs did it!” He pointed to the largest idol still standing among the rubble and said: that one, the one with the muscular arms you carved for him - he did it!

Then came his brilliant challenge: فَسْـَٔلُوهُمْ إِن كَانُوا۟ يَنطِقُونَ - “So ask them, if they can speak!” The word يَنطِقُونَ comes from نطق, which means to utter, to articulate speech. “Ask them - if they have the ability to communicate even one word.”

This echoes a pattern we see throughout Ibrahim’s (AS) methodology. Earlier, when he looked at celestial bodies and declared them his lord, only to reject them when they set, he was demonstrating to people the absurdity of their beliefs by taking their logic to its conclusion. The Quran says: وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَا آتَيْنَاهَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ (And this was Our argument that We gave to Ibrahim). Allah ﷻ gave him these tools of demonstration to expose the foolishness in their beliefs.

Now he was doing the same with the idols. By pointing to the big idol and saying it destroyed the others, he was setting up a logical trap. If they say the idol can’t do that, they admit it’s powerless. If they insist it could have done it, then they acknowledge it’s just an object that clearly didn’t do anything.

Ayah 64

فَرَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ أَنتُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

Something remarkable happened. فَرَجَعُوٓا۟ إِلَىٰٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ - they returned to themselves. They went deep inside themselves. This is what we call soul-searching - literally, the ayah describes them returning into their own selves, looking inward at their own conscience.

And what did their inner voice tell them? فَقَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّكُمْ أَنتُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ - “Indeed, YOU are the wrongdoers!” Notice they didn’t say “we” - they said “you.” It’s as if they were having an out-of-body experience, where their conscience stood apart from them and spoke to them as a separate entity, saying: You are the ones in the wrong.

To understand this with a contemporary image: imagine a cartoon where an angel appears above a character, and the angel tells them they’re wrong. Not a devil with a pitchfork on one shoulder and an angel on the other - in our Islamic understanding, we have two angels who simply record our deeds, one for good actions and one for bad. But in this moment of conscience, it was as though their own soul stepped outside and confronted them with the truth: You are the oppressors, the wrongdoers.

Ayah 65

ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رُءُوسِهِمْ لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يَنطِقُونَ

But then something tragic happened. ثُمَّ نُكِسُوا۟ عَلَىٰ رُءُوسِهِمْ - their heads were made to hang low. The word نُكِسُوا comes from nakasa, which means to lower or hang down. The word is related to nakasul alam - lowering the flag. In times past, a nation’s flag stayed raised, and lowering it signaled defeat and humiliation. To have one’s head hang low means to be in shame.

But notice the passive form: نُكِسُوا - “they were made to hang their heads,” not “they hung their heads.” It’s as if their heads were forced down into shame against their will. There was a moment - a brief, shining moment - where their conscience spoke truth to them. They recognized their error.

But then they snapped back. They couldn’t sustain that moment of honest self-reflection. لَقَدْ عَلِمْتَ مَا هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ يَنطِقُونَ - “You already know that these don’t speak!” They threw the argument back at Ibrahim (AS), breaking free from their moment of shame and returning to their stubbornness. “You already knew they can’t talk at all. You know that!”

Ayah 66

قَالَ أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ

Ibrahim (AS) pressed his point home. أَفَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - “Do you then worship, other than Allah ﷻ…” مَا لَا يَنفَعُكُمْ شَيْـًٔا - “what cannot benefit you in anything at all?” وَلَا يَضُرُّكُمْ - “And cannot harm you either?”

This is the fundamental question of worship. Why would anyone devote themselves to something that has no power to help or harm? These idols cannot bring any benefit - شَيْـًٔا, not even the smallest thing, not anything whatsoever. And they cannot cause harm either. They are completely powerless in every dimension.

Ayah 67

أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ

أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ - this word uff is difficult to translate adequately. It’s an expression of utter disgust and displeasure at something filthy and repulsive. The closest we have in contemporary English might be an expression of revulsion: “How disgusting!” But even that doesn’t fully capture the emotional weight of uff.

It’s the sound you make when you see something utterly vile - like walking past a pile of nail clippings and recoiling in disgust. Uff is that visceral reaction of moral and spiritual repulsion. “How disgusting - you people!” أُفٍّ لَّكُمْ وَلِمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ - “Disgusting are you, and disgusting is what you worship besides Allah ﷻ!”

أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ - “Will you not use your intellect? Will you not think?” The word تَعْقِلُونَ comes from aql - intellect, reason, the ability to think critically and draw proper conclusions. Ibrahim (AS) was appealing to their rationality: What is wrong with you that you cannot think this through? How can you not see the obvious reality before you?

Ayah 68

قَالُوا۟ حَرِّقُوهُ وَٱنصُرُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ

And here we see the final recourse of those who have no argument: violence. قَالُوا۟ حَرِّقُوهُ - “They said: Burn him!” The word حَرِّقُوهُ has intensity to it - not just burn him, but burn him completely, reduce him to nothing. It comes from tahreeq, which implies burning something until even the bones are gone, burning something to complete destruction.

وَٱنصُرُوٓا۟ ءَالِهَتَكُمْ - “And help your gods!” This is the ultimate irony. Ibrahim (AS) had just told them their gods cannot help or harm, cannot benefit or hurt. He demonstrated they couldn’t even speak or defend themselves. And their response? “Help your gods!”

They made Ibrahim’s (AS) argument for him. If these idols need human beings to help them and defend them, what kind of gods are they? They’re so helpless that their worshipers must come to their aid.

This reveals a critical pattern in human discourse: when someone presents truth and you have no rational response, threatening their life becomes the last refuge. It’s one of the most common major logical fallacies. When people appeal to emotions - tradition, family loyalty, national pride - instead of addressing the actual argument, it means they’ve already lost on the merits. When someone says “two plus two equals four” and a bigger person comes along and says “No, it’s five - and I’ll hurt you if you disagree,” might doesn’t make right. But might can force itself to be accepted as right.

The people had no answer for Ibrahim (AS). They couldn’t defend their beliefs rationally. They couldn’t explain why they worshiped powerless idols. They couldn’t justify their ancestral practices. So they resorted to the only thing left: eliminating the person who spoke truth. إِن كُنتُمْ فَـٰعِلِينَ - “If you are going to do anything at all.” In other words: this is your only option. You have nothing else. Burning him is all you can do.

This passage leaves us with powerful lessons about truth and falsehood, about the courage to stand against established norms when they contradict tawhid, and about the bankruptcy of arguments that rely on tradition, emotion and violence instead of reason and evidence. Ibrahim (AS) demonstrated that youth doesn’t disqualify someone from speaking truth, that respect for ancestors cannot supersede commitment to Allah ﷻ, and that false beliefs crumble when confronted with clear reasoning - even if their adherents refuse to accept what their own conscience tells them.

Ayah 69–88 · linked video: 06. Al-Anbya (Ayah 69-88)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 69-88

Ayah 69

قُلْنَا يَـٰنَارُ كُونِى بَرْدًا وَسَلَـٰمًا عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ

We commanded, “O fire! Be cool and safe for Ibrahim.”

The divine command came directly to the fire itself: become cool and peaceful specifically for Ibrahim (AS). Some scholars suggest that Ibrahim (AS) never experienced such profound joy in his entire life as he did within that fire. The coolness and peace he tasted there surpassed anything he had known before. This was the fire turning cold at Allah’s ﷻ command.

Consider the dramatic irony of this moment. Ibrahim (AS) had just declared to his people that their gods possessed no power to harm or benefit anyone. Now, the God of Ibrahim was demonstrating His absolute control by transforming fire into coolness. Historical accounts tell us that when this fire was built, it burned so intensely that birds could not fly over it without falling in. The flames towered so high that Ibrahim’s (AS) people had to construct a catapult just to throw him into it, as they could not approach the inferno directly.

What’s particularly significant is that Allah ﷻ did not inform Ibrahim (AS) beforehand that the fire would become cool. We don’t find in the Quran Allah ﷻ saying to Ibrahim (AS), “Don’t worry, the fire will be cool and peaceful for you.” No such reassurance came. Instead, Allah ﷻ addressed the fire directly. This teaches us a fundamental principle about divine help: Allah’s ﷻ assistance arrives after you demonstrate readiness to make your sacrifice, not before. Allah ﷻ does not provide guarantees in advance that remove the test itself.

The angels appeared at the Battle of Badr, but they weren’t waiting beforehand, asking, “Where have you been?” The Muslims had to arrive first. They had to engage an enemy impossibly more powerful than themselves. Only then did the angels arrive. The pattern is consistent throughout Islamic history: the help of Allah ﷻ does not manifest until the effort from the believer comes first, until the sacrifice from the believer is made.

Ayah 70

وَأَرَادُوا۟ بِهِۦ كَيْدًا فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمُ ٱلْأَخْسَرِينَ

They had intended to harm him through their plot, but We made them the worst losers.

Ibrahim’s (AS) people planned against him with specific intentions. They wanted him to cave under pressure. He was young, and they expected that when he saw the gigantic fire and found himself seated in the catapult, he would pull back and recant. They anticipated he would say something like, “I’ll rebuild all your idols. My father manufactures them, so I know how to make them. I’ll fix everything. I’ll compensate you, I promise.”

But nothing of the sort happened. Their plot failed completely. They actually had to follow through with throwing him into the fire. In doing so, they ended up proving their own religion wrong. We made them the ultimate losers—they demonstrated beyond doubt the powerlessness of their gods and the limitless power of the God of Ibrahim (AS).

Ayah 71

وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَلُوطًا إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

We rescued him and Lut to the land that We had blessed for all nations.

This event must have enraged Ibrahim’s (AS) opponents even more. Yet somehow—and Allah ﷻ keeps the precise mechanism a mystery—We rescued him. Along with him, We rescued his nephew Lut (AS). Lut (AS) must have been much younger, perhaps only four or five years old while Ibrahim (AS) was in his teens. It’s also possible that Lut (AS) was rescued at a later time, and Allah ﷻ grouped their rescues together in this passage.

They left Iraq, they left Babylon, and migrated to a land that We had blessed—the land of Sham, the region of Damascus. The phrase لِلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (for all nations) indicates the universal significance of this blessed land. This is the territory from which many of the great prophets of the world would arise, making it a source of blessing for all humanity.

Ayah 72

وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥٓ إِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ نَافِلَةً ۖ وَكُلًّا جَعَلْنَا صَـٰلِحِينَ

We granted him Ishaq, and Yaqub as an additional gift, and We made all of them righteous.

Allah ﷻ granted Ibrahim (AS) his son Ishaq (AS), and then نَافِلَةً —as an additional, bonus gift—his grandson Yaqub (AS). The word nafilah means something extra, something beyond what was expected or requested. For non-Arabic speakers, nafilah refers to something additional, a bonus.

All of them We made righteous—Salihin. Why does Allah ﷻ mention this sequence right here? There’s a profound lesson embedded in this placement. When you are ready to make the ultimate sacrifice for Allah ﷻ, and you truly make dua for righteous offspring, then Allah ﷻ will grant you righteous children. He will bestow these gifts in ways that exceed your expectations.

Ayah 73

وَجَعَلْنَـٰهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَإِقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ وَإِيتَآءَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا عَـٰبِدِينَ

We made them leaders who guided by Our command. We revealed to them to perform good deeds, establish prayer, and give charity. They were devoted worshippers of Us.

We made them أَئِمَّةً —leaders, imams—who would guide by Our command. This term imam here refers to spiritual and moral leadership. We revealed to them فِعْلَ ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ —to act upon good deeds, to perform righteous actions. This wasn’t merely theoretical knowledge; it was revealed guidance to implement goodness in practical life.

We commanded them to establish prayer—وَإِقَامَ ٱلصَّلَوٰةِ—and to give charity—وَإِيتَآءَ ٱلزَّكَوٰةِ. All of them had been slaves to Us, devoted worshippers of Ours. The word عَـٰبِدِينَ emphasizes their complete devotion and servitude to Allah ﷻ alone.

Ayah 74

وَلُوطًا ءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَرْيَةِ ٱلَّتِى كَانَت تَّعْمَلُ ٱلْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ ۗ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَـٰسِقِينَ

To Lut, We gave judgment and knowledge, and We rescued him from the town that committed filthy acts. They were truly a people of evil, deeply corrupt.

To his nephew Lut (AS), We had given حُكْمًا —firmness, sound judgment, strong decision-making ability—وَعِلْمًا —and knowledge on top of that. We rescued him from a town that used to perform الْخَبَـٰٓئِثَ —filthy, disgusting acts. They were a nation defined by ugliness and evil.

The description intensifies: إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَـٰسِقِينَ—they were a people of evil, so ugly in their corruption, deeply corrupt to their very core. The term قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ indicates a people characterized and defined by wrongdoing, while فَـٰسِقِينَ points to their internal corruption, their rebellion against all moral boundaries.

Ayah 75

وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

We entered him into Our mercy. Indeed, he is among the righteous.

We entered him into Our mercy—وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ. We allowed him to come within the bounds of Our mercy, to experience its protection and blessings. No doubt, he is from الصَّـٰلِحِينَ —those who are righteous, the good people who act with integrity and devotion.

Ayah 76

وَنُوحًا إِذْ نَادَىٰ مِن قَبْلُ فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ مِنَ ٱلْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ

And Nuh, when he called out long before. We responded to him and rescued him and his family from the great distress.

Now begins a quick succession mentioning many great prophets. This section serves as a review of much of what we’ve studied in the Quran until now, with many of these prophets having been discussed in detail earlier.

Nuh (AS)—remember when he called out to Us from way before, from ancient times. He had called and said, as will come later in the Quran, إِنِّي مَغْلُوبٌ فَانتَصِرْ—“I have been overpowered by my people, so help me.” Allah ﷻ makes brief mention of this, saying: Remember when he called Us from way before?

Then We responded to him—فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ. We rescued him and his family—فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥ—from الْكَرْبِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ —a great disturbance, a distress that would bring about terrible calamity, sadness, and uncertainty. This refers, of course, to the flood.

Ayah 77

وَنَصَرْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ

We aided him against the people who denied Our signs. They were a people of evil, so We drowned them all together.

We aided him against the nation—الَّذِينَ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ—those who lied against Our miraculous signs, who rejected Our clear proofs. They too were قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ —a nation of ugly evil, a people defined and marked by wrongdoing. Their nation’s very identity was bound up in their evil actions.

So We drowned them—فَأَغْرَقْنَـٰهُمْ—all together, أَجْمَعِينَ—every single one of them.

Ayah 78

PRIMARY_AYAH: 78

وَدَاوُۥدَ وَسُلَيْمَـٰنَ إِذْ يَحْكُمَانِ فِى ٱلْحَرْثِ إِذْ نَفَشَتْ فِيهِ غَنَمُ ٱلْقَوْمِ وَكُنَّا لِحُكْمِهِمْ شَـٰهِدِينَ

And Dawud and Sulaiman, when both passed judgment concerning the crop, when the sheep of some people grazed in it at night. We were witnesses to their judgment.

Now we come to Dawud (AS) and Sulaiman (AS), when both of them had to pass a verdict about a crop. There’s a fascinating story condensed here. The Arabic uses the verb نَفَشَتْ, which is remarkably specific. Arabic has certain groups of verbs for things that happen at night—بَاتَ means to spend the night, بَيَّتَ means to spend the night planning, to spend the night plotting. And there’s نَفَشَ.

Nafasha refers specifically to sheep breaking out of their barn at night and going grazing without their shepherd—essentially, the sheep going on an unauthorized nighttime adventure. Apparently, this was such a common problem in that era that they developed an actual verb for it.

Here’s what happened: There was a shepherd with his flock of sheep, and there was a farmer who had been working on his farm the entire year. The farm was now fully grown, lush, ready for harvest. During the night, the sheep broke out of their barn, wandered over to the farm, and ate everything. In the morning, the shepherd’s response was essentially, “My sheep ate your farm,” and they brought this dispute to Dawud (AS), whose son Sulaiman (AS)—at that time a prince, not yet the ruler—was present.

Dawud (AS) passed a verdict on the case. Sulaiman (AS), as a young prince, offered a suggestion. His proposal was this: Why don’t we give the sheep temporarily to the farmer whose year of work was lost? Meanwhile, give the shepherd responsibility for taking care of the farm. When the farm is replenished and ready for harvest again, he can return it to the farmer. In the meantime, the shepherd can care for the sheep and benefit from their milk, wool, and other products for a year, providing the farmer with compensation.

We were شَـٰهِدِينَ —witnesses to the decision they made. This case became famous in Islamic legal literature for its wisdom and fairness.

Ayah 79

فَفَهَّمْنَـٰهَا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ ۚ وَكُلًّا ءَاتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا ۚ وَسَخَّرْنَا مَعَ دَاوُۥدَ ٱلْجِبَالَ يُسَبِّحْنَ وَٱلطَّيْرَ ۚ وَكُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ

We made Sulaiman understand it. To each We gave judgment and knowledge. We made the mountains glorify along with Dawud, and the birds too. It is We who did this.

Then We made Sulaiman (AS) understand it—فَفَهَّمْنَـٰهَا سُلَيْمَـٰنَ. Allah ﷻ takes credit for giving Sulaiman (AS) that understanding, that deeper insight into the case. This teaches us that wisdom and understanding are divine gifts.

To every one of them—وَكُلًّا—We had given حُكْمًا —firm decision-making power and strength of character—وَعِلْمًا —and knowledge. Both father and son were granted these qualities.

Now the passage shifts to describe Dawud’s (AS) unique gifts: We had subdued the mountains with Dawud (AS)—وَسَخَّرْنَا مَعَ دَاوُۥدَ ٱلْجِبَالَ. They would declare Allah’s ﷻ perfection—يُسَبِّحْنَ —they would glorify Allah ﷻ. Dawud (AS) had a remarkably beautiful voice. When he would sing the praises of Allah ﷻ, when he would make tahmid and tasbih, the mountains would sing along with him. وَٱلطَّيْرَ —and the birds would join in song.

You might imagine this like a scene from an animated film—a man walking and singing, with mountains echoing his praise and birds chirping in harmony. It sounds like a peaceful, joyful picture. But the very next ayah corrects any overly serene impression we might have of Dawud (AS).

وَكُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ—and We were the ones doing that. This wasn’t Dawud’s (AS) power; it was Allah’s ﷻ power manifesting through him.

Ayah 80

وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ لَّكُمْ لِتُحْصِنَكُم مِّنۢ بَأْسِكُمْ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُمْ شَـٰكِرُونَ

We taught him the craft of making armor for you, to protect you from your violence. Will you then be grateful?

We taught him—وَعَلَّمْنَـٰهُ صَنْعَةَ لَبُوسٍ —the manufacture of armor. The word لَبُوسٍ refers to armor, protective gear. According to some Muslim historians, this was the first time armor technology of this particular type was developed. The knowledge of it was revealed by Allah ﷻ to Dawud (AS).

Dawud (AS) was a khalifah who engaged in many battles. He developed armor that incorporated chains around the neck area so that when an enemy would strike at the neck, the wearer would be protected. Allah ﷻ revealed to him how to develop this protective technology.

لِتُحْصِنَكُم مِّنۢ بَأْسِكُمْ—so that all of you, to this day, may protect yourselves from your wars, from your violence against each other. In other words, We taught Dawud (AS) this craft so that you, today, can take credit for these inventions and their descendants.

Notice how Allah ﷻ suddenly switches to the second person here—لَّكُمْ, “for you”—to make us realize something crucial. The advancements in technology we see now—bulletproof vests, modern armor, all the protective gear we rely on—are enhancements of what Allah ﷻ originally taught. Each generation comes and adds one step, then another step, then another. But if that first step had never been taken, these subsequent developments wouldn’t exist. Everything we have is, in one way or another, a progression of what came before us.

فَهَلْ أَنتُمْ شَـٰكِرُونَ—So will you be grateful? This ayah also teaches us to be grateful for the accomplishments of those who came before us. We may think we’re living in the most technologically advanced age in human history, and perhaps we are. But does that mean we shouldn’t give credit to those who came before us? Should we not study that history and acknowledge their contributions? This ayah calls us to gratitude for the accumulated knowledge of humanity.

Ayah 81

وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ ٱلرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا ۚ وَكُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَـٰلِمِينَ

To Sulaiman We gave the wind, blowing violently, running by his command to the land We had blessed. We are fully aware of all things.

To Sulaiman (AS)—وَلِسُلَيْمَـٰنَ—We gave the winds—ٱلرِّيحَ عَاصِفَةً , winds that blow very strong, violently strong. The word asifah describes wind that doesn’t care about anything in its path, that destroys everything before it, tremendously powerful wind.

تَجْرِى بِأَمْرِهِۦٓ—It would blow by his command. Allah ﷻ gave Sulaiman (AS) control over the wind as a weapon, as an ability. The wind became a tool at his disposal, traveling إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلَّتِى بَـٰرَكْنَا فِيهَا—all the way to the land that We had blessed—the land of Sham once again.

وَكُنَّا بِكُلِّ شَىْءٍ عَـٰلِمِينَ—And We were fully knowledgeable of all things. We knew every detail of what Sulaiman (AS) did with these gifts, every application of these powers.

Ayah 82

وَمِنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُۥ وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ ۖ وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ

Among the devils were those who dived for him and performed other work besides that. We were watchful over them.

وَمِنَ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينِ—And out of the devils—the evil jinn are called شَّيَـٰطِينِ, devils. Even the evil ones, the ones who don’t believe, were subdued by Sulaiman (AS) through Allah’s ﷻ permission.

مَن يَغُوصُونَ لَهُۥ—They would dive for him. They became his submarines, his naval divers. Sulaiman (AS) would send jinn deep into the ocean to retrieve pearls, coral, and various treasures from the ocean floor.

وَيَعْمَلُونَ عَمَلًا دُونَ ذَٰلِكَ—And they would perform all kinds of other work for him besides that. وَكُنَّا لَهُمْ حَـٰفِظِينَ—And We were the guardians, the watchful protectors over all of them. If We had wanted, We could have caused the jinn to drown underwater. But We were the ones watching over them, maintaining their safety even as they served Sulaiman (AS).

Ayah 83

وَأَيُّوبَ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥٓ أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ

And Ayub, when he called upon his Master: “Harm has afflicted me, and You are the Most Merciful of those who show mercy.”

The Quran continues its quick succession of prophets, now turning to Ayub (AS), an ancient prophet. The special quality that defined his lifetime was that Allah ﷻ tested him with all kinds of diseases, one after another in succession—very difficult physical ailments.

Allah ﷻ describes his prayer: إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥٓ—When he called on his Master—أَنِّى مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ—“Harm has touched me.” Notice the careful wording. He’s talking to Allah ﷻ, but he doesn’t say, “You made me sick.” He says, “Harm has touched me”—مَسَّنِىَ ٱلضُّرُّ. He doesn’t even directly mention the sickness, doesn’t attribute it explicitly to Allah ﷻ.

And immediately after mentioning his difficulty, he says: وَأَنتَ أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ—“And You are the Most Merciful of all who show mercy.”

This is remarkable. The sick person doesn’t make dua saying, “Come on, enough already! Remove this fever! You brought it, You can take it away!” No. Ayub (AS) says, “The harm has touched me, and You—You are the Most Merciful of all who show mercy.”

What happened to Ayub (AS) was severe. He became so sick, developing sores and pox all over his skin, that his own family couldn’t touch him. Eventually, they abandoned him, running away from him. Even in that state of utter abandonment and physical suffering, he turned to Allah ﷻ and said, “You are the Most Merciful.” Not “You could be more merciful” or “Please be more merciful,” but “You ARE أَرْحَمُ ٱلرَّٰحِمِينَ—the Most Merciful of all who show mercy.”

Ayah 84

فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ فَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِۦ مِن ضُرٍّ ۖ وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَهْلَهُۥ وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰبِدِينَ

We responded to him and removed all the harm he suffered. We gave him back his family and the like of them with them, as a mercy from Us and a reminder for the worshippers.

فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ—Then We responded to him. فَكَشَفْنَا مَا بِهِۦ مِن ضُرٍّ—Then We alleviated, We removed any of the diseases, any of the harms and pains that he suffered. Every trace of his illness was lifted.

وَءَاتَيْنَـٰهُ أَهْلَهُۥ—And We gave him his family back. وَمِثْلَهُم مَّعَهُمْ—and the like of them along with them. This could mean his wife remarried him after leaving, or it could mean he had more children—another family equal to or like the one he had before, along with the original family.

رَحْمَةً مِّنْ عِندِنَا—as a mercy that came especially from Us, a mercy from Our special attention. وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰبِدِينَ—and as a powerful reminder for those who worship.

When you know family members or friends who fall sick, or when you yourself are ill, this is a powerful reminder for those who worship Allah ﷻ. The phrase وَذِكْرَىٰ لِلْعَـٰبِدِينَ emphasizes that this story exists as a living lesson for the worshippers of Allah ﷻ in every generation who face illness and hardship.

Ayahs 85-86

وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِدْرِيسَ وَذَا ٱلْكِفْلِ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ

وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

And Ismail, Idris, and Dhul-Kifl—each was among the patient ones. We entered them into Our mercy. Indeed, they are among the righteous.

Now Allah ﷻ mentions three more prophets together: Ismail (AS), Idris (AS), and Dhul-Kifl. These are mentioned briefly, grouped together by a common quality.

Regarding Dhul-Kifl, we know very little. There is virtually no hadith about him, no detailed accounts of his life. An interesting theory exists, proposed by scholar Hamza Yusuf Hanson, that Dhul-Kifl may actually be the Buddha, and that his teachings were later corrupted and transformed into what became Buddhism. He wrote a paper presenting evidence for this theory.

His reasoning includes the linguistic connection: Dhul-Kifl’s town was called Kifl, and Kifl in pronunciation bears similarity to certain forms of Buddha’s name. Additionally, some of the core teachings associated with the historical Buddha—reliance on the divine, patience through suffering, and other spiritual principles—align with prophetic teachings that may have survived but became corrupted, enhanced, and changed over time. Many ancient prophets experienced this fate: their teachings survived but became distorted into other religious systems. This theory remains speculative, but it’s worth noting.

What we know for certain is: كُلٌّ مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ—all of them were from those who were patient, from the صَّـٰبِرِينَ. Interestingly, what inclined some scholars toward the Buddha theory is this very mention of patience, since the historical Buddha is known for the tremendous torture he endured and the patience he maintained through it.

وَأَدْخَلْنَـٰهُمْ فِى رَحْمَتِنَآ—We entered them into Our mercy. إِنَّهُم مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ—No doubt they are from the righteous, from those who act with integrity and devotion.

Ayah 87

وَذَا ٱلنُّونِ إِذ ذَّهَبَ مُغَـٰضِبًا فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ فَنَادَىٰ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ أَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنتَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ إِنِّى كُنتُ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

And Dhun-Nun, when he left in anger, thinking We would not restrict him. Then he called out in the depths of darkness: “There is no god but You. Glory be to You. I was indeed among the wrongdoers.”

وَذَا ٱلنُّونِ—And the person of the whale, the “whale guy.” Who is this? It’s Yunus (AS), called here the possessor of the whale, which is interesting because the whale actually possessed him, swallowing him whole.

نُونِ is the Arabic word for whale or large fish. There’s a fascinating connection in Arabic calligraphy and ancient Egyptian hieroglyphics: when a fish is pulled out of water, it curves and bends, and if you imagine a drop of water on top of that curve, you get the shape of the Arabic letter ن (nun)—the bend with a dot, representing the fish. The letter itself may derive from this imagery.

It’s also worth noting that the Surah that begins with نٓ - Surah Al-Qalam—ends with mention of صَاحِبِ ٱلْحُوتِ, another title for Yunus (AS), creating an interesting parallel structure.

إِذ ذَّهَبَ مُغَـٰضِبًا—When he went away angry. The word مُغَـٰضِبًا is particularly significant. If he were simply angry, the Quran would use غَاضِبًا. But the form مُغَـٰضِبًا is mufa’ala form in Arabic, which indicates mutual action. This means he was angry at his people, and they were angry at him. It was mutual anger, mutual frustration.

فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ—And he thought that We would not have power over him. The phrase نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ doesn’t mean he thought Allah ﷻ lacked power. Rather, it means he didn’t think Allah ﷻ would hold him accountable, wouldn’t restrict him, wouldn’t punish him. He felt justified in his anger and his departure, not recognizing it as wrong.

فَنَادَىٰ فِى ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ—Then he made a call in the depths of darkness, in the ظُّلُمَـٰتِ —plural darknesses. This refers to being in the belly of the whale. Imagine the scene: Who can hear you in the belly of a whale? Who’s going to hear your cries from there?

أَن لَّآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّآ أَنتَ—“There is no one worthy of worship or obedience except You.” سُبْحَـٰنَكَ—“How perfect You are! How far removed You are from any imperfection!” إِنِّى كُنتُ مِنَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ—“I, no doubt, am the one who is from the wrongdoers, from those who wrong themselves.”

So here we’re learning the dua of someone who is imprisoned, who finds himself trapped in the most impossible situation. This becomes one of the most powerful supplications in Islam, teaching us that no matter how dark our circumstances, no matter how trapped we feel, we can always call on Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 88

فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُـۨجِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ

We responded to him and rescued him from his distress. That is how We rescue the believers.

فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ—Then We responded to him. Imagine: who can hear you in the belly of a whale, in the depths of the ocean? Yet We responded to him.

وَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ مِنَ ٱلْغَمِّ—And We rescued him from الْغَمِّ —the overwhelming distress, the dark sadness and anxiety. Earlier we talked about ghamm being darkness, and here it’s literally dark too—the darkness inside the whale’s belly—but it’s also the darkness of despair and distress.

وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُـۨجِى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ—And that is exactly how We immediately rescue the believers. This is the pattern, this is the way: when believers call on Allah ﷻ sincerely, acknowledging His perfection and their own failings, that is how We rescue المؤمنين —those who truly believe.

This final statement transforms Yunus’s (AS) story from a historical account into a living promise for every believer. The same Allah ﷻ who heard the call from the belly of the whale hears every believer’s call from whatever darkness they find themselves in. This is how We rescue the believers—not just Yunus (AS), but all who believe and call upon Allah ﷻ with sincerity and humility.

Ayah 89–112 · linked video: 07. Al-Anbya (Ayah 89-112)

Surah Al-Anbiya: Ayahs 89-112

Ayah 89

وَزَكَرِيَّآ إِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبَّهُۥ رَبِّ لَا تَذَرْنِى فَرْدًا وَأَنتَ خَيْرُ ٱلْوَٰرِثِينَ

After mentioning several prophets in succession, the surah continues with the account of Zakariya (AS). Each prophet mentioned shares a common thread: they called upon their Master in times of need. Zakariya (AS) made a deeply personal supplication: “Rabbi, la tadharnī fardā” - Master, don’t leave me by myself, don’t leave me without an heir.

His request reflects a beautiful understanding of Allah’s ﷻ sovereignty. When he says “wa anta khayrul wārithīn” (and You are the best of those who inherit), he acknowledges that ultimately, Allah ﷻ will inherit everyone and everything. Yet even with this knowledge, Zakariya (AS) still expresses his human desire for someone to inherit him in this worldly life. This demonstrates that turning to Allah ﷻ with our deepest wishes is not only permissible but encouraged, even when we recognize His ultimate ownership of all things.

Ayah 90

فَٱسْتَجَبْنَا لَهُۥ وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُۥ يَحْيَىٰ وَأَصْلَحْنَا لَهُۥ زَوْجَهُۥٓ ۚ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يُسَـٰرِعُونَ فِى ٱلْخَيْرَٰتِ وَيَدْعُونَنَا رَغَبًا وَرَهَبًا ۖ وَكَانُوا۟ لَنَا خَـٰشِعِينَ

Allah ﷻ responded to Zakariya’s (AS) supplication in multiple ways. First, He granted him Yahya (AS) as a gift. The word “wahab” (granted) emphasizes that this was a pure gift from Allah ﷻ, not something earned or deserved, but bestowed out of His generosity.

Then Allah ﷻ says “wa aslahna lahu zawjahu” - and We fixed, reconciled, or made right his spouse. This is linguistically significant. In the Quran, when something is absent from a marriage - whether harmony, a child, or completeness - the word “imra’ah” is typically used. But when everything is present and the marriage is whole, the word “zawj” appears. The only exceptions are the wives of the Prophet ﷺ (azwajul mutahharāt) and the wife of Adam (AS), both cases where nothing was lacking. Here, after granting Zakariya (AS) a child, Allah ﷻ switches from “imra’ah” (used elsewhere when describing her before the child) to “zawj,” indicating the completion and wholeness she now experiences.

The ayah concludes by explaining why this family deserved such blessings. “Innahum kānū yusāri’ūna fil khayrāt” - they used to compete in good deeds, racing toward righteousness. Their supplications combined two essential elements: “raghaban wa rahaban” - hope and fear. They called upon Allah ﷻ with eager hope for His mercy and profound fear of His displeasure. This balance in worship is crucial - not being paralyzed by fear alone, nor becoming complacent through hope alone.

The ayah ends with “wa kānū lanā khāshi’īn” - and they were exclusively fearful of Us, exclusively humble before Us. Their reverence and awe were reserved for Allah ﷻ alone, not diluted by fear of creation or concern for people’s opinions.

Ayah 91

وَٱلَّتِىٓ أَحْصَنَتْ فَرْجَهَا فَنَفَخْنَا فِيهَا مِن رُّوحِنَا وَجَعَلْنَـٰهَا وَٱبْنَهَآ ءَايَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

The narrative shifts to Maryam (AS), described as “allatī ahsanat farjahā” - the one whose chastity We protected. The word “farj” literally means an opening or gap, used here to refer to her private modesty with the utmost dignity and respect. Allah ﷻ Himself testifies to her purity and protection.

“Fanafakhnā fīhā min rūhinā” - then We blew into her from Our spirit. This refers to the angel Jibril (AS) breathing into her by Allah’s ﷻ command, resulting in the miraculous conception of Isa (AS). The attribution of the spirit to Allah ﷻ (“min rūhinā” - from Our spirit) indicates honor and elevation, similar to how the Kaaba is called “baytullah” (the House of Allah).

“Wa ja’alnāhā wabnaha āyatan lil ’ālamīn” - and We made her and her son a sign for all the worlds. The phrase “lil ’ālamīn” (for all the worlds) is significant. The miraculous nature of Isa’s (AS) birth is one miracle, but the reference to “all worlds” extends to his miraculous return. When he descends near the end of times, he will confirm the prophethood of Muhammad ﷺ and serve as a sign for all nations. The Jews will witness that his mother was indeed virtuous, withdrawing their false accusations. The Christians will recognize that neither he nor his mother were part of any trinity, but rather honored servants of Allah ﷻ. At that time, Maryam (AS) will truly become the sign she was always meant to be for all of humanity.

Ayah 92

إِنَّ هَـٰذِهِۦٓ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَٰحِدَةً وَأَنَا۠ رَبُّكُمْ فَٱعْبُدُونِ

After recounting the stories of multiple prophets across different eras and lands, Allah ﷻ makes a unifying declaration: “Inna hādhihi ummatukum ummatan wāhidah” - indeed, this is your nation, a single nation. What a remarkable statement. Despite the vast spans of time and geography separating these prophets, they belong to one united community.

The connection becomes clear: these prophets, each in their unique circumstances, called upon their Master. Nuh (AS) called out when drowning in his people’s rejection. Ibrahim (AS) called out for protection and guidance. Ayyub (AS) called out in illness. Yunus (AS) called out from the belly of the whale. Zakariya (AS) called out for an heir. Now, the believers who hear these stories are told: you belong to this group. You are part of this same spiritual nation that stretches across human history.

“Wa ana rabbukum fa’budūn” - and I am your Master, so worship Me alone. The unity of the Ummah is not merely horizontal (with each other) but vertical (with all the prophets who came before). Being one Ummah means sharing in this heritage of calling upon Allah ﷻ and worshiping Him alone. Enslaving ourselves to Allah ﷻ is inseparable from being united as one community.

Some scholars have understood this ayah as a culmination: after all that history, you have finally arrived. This is your time, your Ummah. You are now a single unified nation, so dedicate yourselves to His worship.

Ayah 93

وَتَقَطَّعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَهُم بَيْنَهُمْ ۖ كُلٌّ إِلَيْنَا رَٰجِعُونَ

Despite the call to unity, Allah ﷻ observes a troubling reality: “wa taqatta’ū amrahum baynahum” - they cut up the matter among themselves. They took the religion and sliced it into pieces, each group claiming a portion. The word “taqatta’ū” evokes the image of something whole being deliberately torn apart.

This manifests in various ways throughout history. Some people focus exclusively on loving the Prophet ﷺ, making it their entire religion. Others obsess over halal and haram food regulations, as if Islam consists of nothing else. Some immerse themselves in Tasawwuf and forget other obligations. Others dedicate themselves solely to Aqeedah and neglect practical worship. Everyone picks their favorite aspect of Islam and makes it the totality of their practice.

In modern times, this fragmentation takes the form of competing organizations and ideologies. The existence of multiple Muslim organizations is not inherently problematic - in fact, we should encourage diverse efforts and causes. The problem arises when these organizations view each other as competition rather than allies, when they engage in backbiting, undercutting, or resenting each other’s success. When one group thinks, “They’re getting more people, we’re getting fewer,” the disease of division has taken root.

“Kullun ilaynā rāji’ūn” - all of them will return to Us. Regardless of these divisions, everyone will ultimately stand before Allah ﷻ individually. The groups and factions won’t matter then. Each person will be held accountable for their own deeds.

Ayah 94

فَمَن يَعْمَلْ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَهُوَ مُؤْمِنٌ فَلَا كُفْرَانَ لِسَعْيِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَهُۥ كَـٰتِبُونَ

This ayah offers tremendous reassurance: “faman ya’mal minas sālihāti wa huwa mu’min” - whoever does any righteous deeds while maintaining Iman. The word “min” (from among) is significant - it means any good deed, any righteous action, any positive contribution. There are countless ways to serve, numerous good causes to support, and infinite opportunities to do good.

“Falā kufrāna lisa’yihi” - then there will be no denial of his effort. The word “kufrān” comes from the root meaning to cover up, bury, or reject. Allah ﷻ promises that no effort will be buried, forgotten, dismissed, or denied. Every good deed, no matter how small it seems, will be acknowledged and appreciated.

“Wa innā lahu kātibūn” - and We are certainly recording it for him. Allah ﷻ Himself documents every action. As mentioned elsewhere in the Quran regarding those who migrate for His sake: not a footstep goes unrecorded. Not a single rotation of your car’s wheels driving toward a good deed escapes documentation. Not one step taken toward the masjid for prayer is lost. Everything is preserved, nothing is wasted.

This ayah provides liberation from the anxiety that our efforts might go unnoticed or unappreciated. Even if people ignore or belittle your contributions, even if you never receive worldly recognition, Allah ﷻ is recording everything with perfect accuracy and will reward it accordingly.

Ayah 95

وَحَرَٰمٌ عَلَىٰ قَرْيَةٍ أَهْلَكْنَـٰهَآ أَنَّهُمْ لَا يَرْجِعُونَ

The tone shifts dramatically: “wa harāmun ’alā qaryatin ahlaknāhā annahum lā yarji’ūn” - and it is forbidden for a town that We have destroyed that they should return. Once Allah’s ﷻ decree of destruction falls upon a people, there is no coming back for them in this worldly life.

This serves as a stark warning to the Quraysh and to all who hear this message. Allah ﷻ is essentially saying: I haven’t destroyed you yet. But understand this - once that destruction comes, there will be no second chances in this world, no opportunity to rebuild or start over. The finality should inspire urgency in seeking guidance while the door remains open.

The message is clear: use your time wisely. Accept the truth while you still can. Don’t wait until it’s too late, because once Allah’s ﷻ judgment falls, that chapter closes permanently.

Ayah 96

حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا فُتِحَتْ يَأْجُوجُ وَمَأْجُوجُ وَهُم مِّن كُلِّ حَدَبٍ يَنسِلُونَ

This state of affairs will continue “hattā idhā futihat Ya’jūju wa Ma’jūj” - until Ya’juj and Ma’juj are released. These mysterious peoples represent one of the major signs of the approaching Day of Judgment. They are mentioned in Surah Al-Kahf in the story of Dhul-Qarnayn (AS), who built a barrier to contain them.

Classical scholars acknowledged significant uncertainty about Ya’juj and Ma’juj. Many admitted they didn’t know whether they were human, where exactly the barrier was located, or when they would emerge. This humility in knowledge is important - not everything in the Quran requires definitive identification in our current reality.

Contemporary scholars have various theories. Some point to remnants of copper walls in the Balkan mountain ranges. In recent centuries, certain Indian and Arab scholars living under colonization theorized that Europeans represented Ya’juj and Ma’juj, since they emerged from the mountainous regions where Asia meets Europe, descended upon Muslim lands, plundered resources, and then receded. The famous poet Iqbal even incorporated this interpretation into his poetry, using the phrase “min kulli hadabin yansilūn” from this ayah.

While such interpretations reflect the context of their times, the important point is that Islamic thought is necessarily influenced by the age and society in which we live. Every generation brings its experiences and knowledge to their understanding of revelation. We must maintain the established principles of interpretation while recognizing that human intellects vary, which is why scholarly interpretations vary across times and places.

“Wahum min kulli hadabin yansilūn” - and they will be descending from every elevation. The word “hadab” refers to any rise or height in the earth - hills, mountains, elevated ground. The image is of a massive force pouring down from every highland, an overwhelming descent that cannot be stopped. This emergence of Ya’juj and Ma’juj signals that the end times are imminent.

Ayah 97

وَٱقْتَرَبَ ٱلْوَعْدُ ٱلْحَقُّ فَإِذَا هِىَ شَـٰخِصَةٌ أَبْصَـٰرُ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ يَـٰوَيْلَنَا قَدْ كُنَّا فِى غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا بَلْ كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ

“Waqtarabal wa’dul haqq” - and the true promise has drawn near. After the emergence of Ya’juj and Ma’juj, the Day of Judgment itself approaches rapidly. Everything accelerates toward that inevitable moment.

“Fa idhā hiya shākhisatun absārul ladhīna kafarū” - then suddenly, the eyes of those who disbelieved will be completely petrified. The word “shākhisah” describes eyes that are frozen open, unable to blink, staring in absolute shock and horror. Imagine being so overwhelmed by what you see that you cannot even close your eyelids. This is the state of the disbelievers when reality finally hits them.

“Yā waylanā” - O our destruction! This is an expression of utter despair and regret. They cry out: “qad kunnā fī ghaflatin min hādhā” - we were completely heedless of this, completely oblivious. We spent our entire lives in denial, treating these warnings as myths or fairy tales.

But then comes the crucial admission: “bal kunnā dhālimīn” - no, actually, we were wrongdoers. They correct themselves. It wasn’t just that they were heedless or ignorant - they were criminals. They had the evidence, they heard the warnings, they saw the signs, but they actively chose to reject and oppose the truth. The problem wasn’t lack of information; it was willful injustice against their own souls.

This progression shows that when the truth becomes undeniable, even the most stubborn disbelievers will acknowledge their fault. But tragically, this acknowledgment comes too late to benefit them.

Ayah 98

إِنَّكُمْ وَمَا تَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ حَصَبُ جَهَنَّمَ أَنتُمْ لَهَا وَٰرِدُونَ

The announcement continues: “Innakum wa mā ta’budūna min dūnillāhi hasabu jahannnam” - all of you and whatever you worshiped besides Allah are fuel for Hellfire. The word “hasab” is particularly striking. While “hatab” refers to logs of wood thrown into fire, “hasab” describes the smaller pieces - coal, stones, pebbles - that burn intensely and are shoveled into flames. These tiny burning pieces can fuel a fire for extended periods.

The imagery reveals the scale of Hellfire. Compared to its vastness, even human beings are reduced to mere pebbles. You and your false gods together amount to nothing more than small bits of fuel, insignificant in size yet sufficient to keep the fire burning.

“Antum lahā waridūn” - you will be thrust into it, inserted into it. The word “warid” implies being made to enter, being brought to something. There’s no escape, no negotiation, no alternative. Both the worshipers and the worshiped will be cast in together.

Ayah 99

لَوْ كَانَ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ ءَالِهَةً مَّا وَرَدُوهَا ۖ وَكُلٌّ فِيهَا خَـٰلِدُونَ

“Law kāna hā’ulā’i ālihatan mā waradūhā” - if these things you worshiped had actually been gods, they would never have been entered into it. This is devastating logic. A true god would have power, would have authority, would be able to protect itself and its followers. But these false deities are as helpless as their worshipers, being thrown into the same fire, unable to save themselves or anyone else.

“Wa kullun fīhā khālidūn” - and all of them will remain in it forever. The word “khālidūn” emphasizes permanence and eternity. There is no temporary sentence here, no time off for good behavior, no eventual release. Both the false gods and their worshipers will be trapped together in that state forever, a permanent reminder of the futility of shirk (associating partners with Allah ﷻ).

Ayah 100

لَهُمْ فِيهَا زَفِيرٌ وَهُمْ فِيهَا لَا يَسْمَعُونَ

“Lahum fīhā zafīr” - for them in it there will be loud, painful exhaling. The word “zafīr” describes a specific sound - the progressively louder groaning of a mule or donkey as the load on its back increases and the pain intensifies. It’s not a quick cry but a sustained, escalating sound of agony that builds and builds.

The Quran describes Hellfire itself in multiple places as a living creature with frightening qualities. It has an inhale and an exhale. It speaks and makes requests. It expresses hunger and asks Allah ﷻ for more fuel. The word “zafīr” is linguistically only used for sounds made by living things, reinforcing this description of Hell as not merely a place but a terrifying creature.

“Wahum fīhā lā yasma’ūn” - and they will not hear in it. This is particularly cruel. The false gods that people relied on will be right next to them in the fire. The worshipers will hear the screams and the agony all around them, but their gods - the very beings they trusted for help - won’t hear anything. They’ll be completely deaf and useless, unable to respond to the desperate cries of their former devotees. The utter disappointment of realizing that everything you depended on is powerless and unresponsive adds psychological torment to physical suffering.

Ayah 101

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا ٱلْحُسْنَىٰٓ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ

The scene shifts dramatically to the believers: “Inna alladhīna sabaqat lahum minnal husnā” - indeed, those for whom the best has already preceded from Us. This refers to people for whom Allah ﷻ has already decreed goodness, already written success, already prepared the best of outcomes.

The word “sabaqat” (preceded) is beautiful - before they were even born, before they performed a single deed, Allah’s ﷻ good decree for them was already in place. This speaks to divine foreknowledge and predestination working in harmony with human choice and action.

“Ulā’ika ’anhā mub’adūn” - those will be distanced far away from it. While the previous ayahs described people being thrust into Hellfire, unable to escape, these believers are taken in the opposite direction - far, far away from any danger or harm. The distance itself becomes a mercy.

Ayah 102

لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا ۖ وَهُمْ فِى مَا ٱشْتَهَتْ أَنفُسُهُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ

“Lā yasma’ūna hasīsahā” - they will not even hear its whisper, its faint sound. The word “hasīs” refers to the subtle sound of footsteps, or the barely audible crackling of a flame. Even this faintest hint of Hellfire will be completely inaudible to them. They won’t just be saved from it - they won’t even be aware of its existence. Complete peace and security.

“Wahum fī mashtahat anfusuhum khālidūn” - and they will be in whatever their souls desire, remaining there eternally. The phrase “mashtahat anfusuhum” (whatever their souls desire) is unlimited. Anything that crosses their minds, any wish or whim, will be instantly fulfilled. Imagine thinking, “I wish I had wings,” and suddenly you do. Whatever thought enters your head becomes your reality.

And unlike the temporary pleasures of this world, this state is “khālidūn” - eternal and permanent. The same word used for the eternal punishment of disbelievers is now used for the eternal bliss of believers, but in a completely opposite context.

Ayah 103

لَا يَحْزُنُهُمُ ٱلْفَزَعُ ٱلْأَكْبَرُ وَتَتَلَقَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى كُنتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ

“Lā yahzunuhumul faza’ul akbar” - the greatest terror will not grieve them. This “greatest terror” refers to the first blowing of the trumpet, when all creation will perish. According to hadith narrated by the Prophet ﷺ, before this moment arrives, Allah ﷻ will send a wind that will gently take the souls of all believers from the earth. Not a single believer will remain on the planet.

After the believers are removed, life will continue for those who remain. They will think they’ve finally achieved their post-modern utopia, free from what they considered the primitive burden of religion. They’ll believe life is perfect. Then, suddenly and without warning, the Day of Judgment will arrive. But the believers won’t experience this terror because they won’t even be present for it. They will have already been taken to safety.

“Wa tatalaqqāhumul malā’ikah” - and the angels will greet them closely. The word “tatalaqqā” implies meeting with warmth and welcome, receiving someone with honor. These aren’t distant, formal angels - they’re approaching the believers with familiarity and joy.

“Hādhā yawmukumul ladhī kuntum tū’adūn” - this is your day that you were promised. Even today, we use this phrase for celebrations. We say it at graduations, promotions, weddings - “This is your day!” The angels are telling the believers: after all your patience, your struggles, your sacrifices, your worship - this is YOUR day to celebrate. Everything you were promised has arrived, and it’s all for you.

Ayah 104

يَوْمَ نَطْوِى ٱلسَّمَآءَ كَطَىِّ ٱلسِّجِلِّ لِلْكُتُبِ ۚ كَمَا بَدَأْنَآ أَوَّلَ خَلْقٍ نُّعِيدُهُۥ ۚ وَعْدًا عَلَيْنَآ ۚ إِنَّا كُنَّا فَـٰعِلِينَ

“Yawma natwis samā’a katawwiis sijilli lil kutub” - the Day when We will fold up the sky like the folding of scrolls for books. In ancient times, judges would have long scrolls containing the names of criminals and their verdicts. At the end of the day, they would roll up these scrolls and tie them. Allah ﷻ describes folding the entire sky - the vast universe - with the same ease as a judge rolling up a scroll.

The imagery is staggering. The sky, which appears infinite to us, which contains billions of galaxies, will be rolled up like a piece of paper. And in this metaphor, Allah ﷻ is the Judge, and the universe itself is His scroll. Everything we think is permanent and solid will be rolled up and put away.

“Kamā bada’nā awwala khalqin nu’īduh” - as We began the first creation, We will bring it back again. The One who created everything from nothing can certainly recreate it. If the initial creation was possible - and clearly it was, since we exist - then recreation is not only possible but easier. Allah ﷻ is emphasizing that resurrection is not some fanciful impossibility, but a logical certainty given His power.

“Wa’dan ’alaynā innā kunnā fā’ilīn” - this is a promise mandated upon Us, and We have always been those who do what We say. This isn’t a small promise or a casual statement. Allah ﷻ is saying: this was always the plan. From the very beginning of creation, the Day of Resurrection was already decided. This isn’t some surprise development or last-minute decision - it’s been part of the design from eternity. And when Allah ﷻ makes a promise, it will certainly be fulfilled, because He has always been and will always be One who does what He says.

Ayah 105

وَلَقَدْ كَتَبْنَا فِى ٱلزَّبُورِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ٱلذِّكْرِ أَنَّ ٱلْأَرْضَ يَرِثُهَا عِبَادِىَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحُونَ

“Walaqad katabnā fiz Zabūri min ba’didh dhikri annal arda yarithuhā ’ibādiyyas sālihūn” - and We certainly wrote in the Zabur (Psalms), after the earlier Reminder, that the earth will be inherited by My righteous servants.

The Zabur refers to the Psalms given to Dawud (AS), a book filled with remembrance and praise of Allah ﷻ. But on top of all that remembrance and glorification, Allah ﷻ had also documented this crucial promise: the righteous servants will inherit the earth.

Some scholars interpret this as referring to the earth being transformed after resurrection. When Allah ﷻ recreates creation as mentioned in the previous ayah, He will redefine and stretch out this earth. It will be emptied on the Day of Judgment, then transformed into a form of Paradise where righteous servants will ultimately dwell. The inheritance isn’t just spiritual - it’s physical and eternal.

This promise has profound implications for how believers should view their role in the world. We’re not meant to be perpetual victims or permanent underdogs. The ultimate victory and inheritance belong to those who maintain righteousness and servitude to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 106

إِنَّ فِى هَـٰذَا لَبَلَـٰغًا لِّقَوْمٍ عَـٰبِدِينَ

“Inna fī hādhā la balāghal liqawmin ’ābidīn” - indeed, in all of this there is a powerful communication, a sufficient message for a people who truly engage in worship.

The word “balāgh” means a message that reaches its destination effectively, a communication that hits its mark. Everything mentioned in this surah - the stories of the prophets, the descriptions of Judgment Day, the promises of reward and punishment - all of it constitutes a complete and sufficient message.

But this message particularly resonates with “qawmin ’ābidīn” - people who worship, people who are engaged in true servitude to Allah ﷻ. Those whose hearts are already inclined toward Him will find in these ayahs everything they need for guidance, motivation, comfort and direction. The message is there for everyone, but those who approach it with humility and genuine desire to submit will extract the most benefit from it.

Ayah 107

وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَـٰكَ إِلَّا رَحْمَةً لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

“Wa mā arsalnāka illā rahmatal lil’ālamīn” - and We did not send you except as a mercy for all the worlds, for all peoples, for all of humanity.

This is one of the most comprehensive descriptions of the Prophet’s ﷺ mission. He wasn’t sent to one tribe or one nation. He wasn’t sent for one time period or one generation. He was sent as “rahmah” - mercy itself - for “’ālamīn” - all worlds, all peoples, all of creation.

The message he brought contains the guidance that saves people from eternal destruction. The warnings he delivered are themselves mercy, because they allow people to correct course before it’s too late. Even the descriptions of Hellfire are mercy, because they motivate change while change is still possible. Everything about his mission, his message, and his example is designed to be mercy for those who accept it.

This ayah should transform how we view the Prophet ﷺ and his message. Every teaching, every prohibition, every command exists as an expression of divine mercy reaching out to humanity through the final messenger.

Ayah 108

قُلْ إِنَّمَا يُوحَىٰٓ إِلَىَّ أَنَّمَآ إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۖ فَهَلْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ

“Qul innamā yūhā ilayya annamā ilāhukum ilāhun wāhid” - say, the only thing revealed to me, the essence of what has been communicated to me is that your deity, the One to be worshiped and obeyed, can only be one.

This is the summary, the core message, the fundamental truth that everything else builds upon. Strip away all the details, all the stories, all the laws - what remains is this simple, profound declaration: your Ilah (deity worthy of worship) is Wahid (one and only one).

“Fahal antum muslimūn” - so are you going to submit, or what? Will you give up your resistance? The word “muslim” comes from the same root as “aslama” (to surrender, to give up). Allah ﷻ is essentially asking: are you going to quit fighting against the truth? Are you going to submit to reality? Are you going to give up your shirk, your stubbornness, your false gods, and simply surrender to the obvious truth that there is only One worthy of worship?

The question hangs in the air, demanding an answer from every person who hears it.

Ayah 109

فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَقُلْ ءَاذَنتُكُمْ عَلَىٰ سَوَآءٍ ۖ وَإِنْ أَدْرِىٓ أَقَرِيبٌ أَم بَعِيدٌ مَّا تُوعَدُونَ

“Fa in tawallaw faqul ādhan tukum ‘alā sawā’” - then if they turn away, tell them: I have given you all fair warning, I’ve made you all listen equally. The phrase “ādhantukum” comes from “udhn” (ear), meaning I’ve filled your ears with the message. I’ve made sure you heard me clearly and completely. No one can claim ignorance.

“‘Alā sawā’” - on equal terms, plainly, fairly. Everyone received the same message, the same opportunity, the same warning. No one was excluded or given less information. The playing field was level.

“Wa in adrī a qarībun am ba’īdun mā tū’adūn” - and I have no idea whether what you’re being promised is near or far. The Prophet ﷺ is instructed to acknowledge his human limitation. He doesn’t know when the Day of Judgment will arrive. He can’t tell them if it’s imminent or distant. What he can tell them with certainty is that it will happen.

This is fascinating because the Quraysh were thinking among themselves about when this Day of Judgment would come, but they hadn’t even vocalized the question. When the ayah was revealed addressing their unspoken thought, they were shocked - how did he know what we were thinking? The next ayah provides the answer.

Ayah 110

إِنَّهُۥ يَعْلَمُ ٱلْجَهْرَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْلِ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا تَكْتُمُونَ

“Innahu ya’lamul jahra minal qawli wa ya’lamu mā taktumūn” - indeed, He knows what you say openly, and He knows what you hide.

This addresses their unspoken question about how the Prophet ﷺ knew their private thoughts. Allah ﷻ hears everything you say out loud. But He also knows everything you conceal in your hearts, every secret conversation, every hidden thought. Nothing is outside His knowledge.

The disbelievers thought their internal skepticism was private and undetectable. They assumed their whispered doubts among themselves were secret. But Allah ﷻ is informing them: I hear it all, I know it all. Your secret thoughts are as clear to Me as your public declarations.

Ayah 111

وَإِنْ أَدْرِى لَعَلَّهُۥ فِتْنَةٌ لَّكُمْ وَمَتَـٰعٌ إِلَىٰ حِينٍ

“Wa in adrī la’allahu fitnatul lakum wa matā’un ilā hīn” - and I don’t even know if perhaps this delay is a test for you, and you’re just being given temporary enjoyment for a time.

The Prophet ﷺ is acknowledging another possibility. Perhaps the reason the Day of Judgment hasn’t arrived yet, perhaps the delay itself, is “fitnah” for them - a trial and test. They’re being given more time, more chances, more opportunities. But this delay could also be what condemns them, because they interpret it as proof that the warnings aren’t real.

“Wa matā’un ilā hīn” - and temporary enjoyment for a limited time. They’re living comfortable lives, engaging in business, enjoying their wealth, and telling themselves that everything is fine. But this is just temporary provision for a limited duration. The comfort they feel now doesn’t prove anything except that their time hasn’t run out yet.

This ayah contains subtle warning: don’t mistake divine patience for divine approval. Don’t interpret the delay of punishment as evidence that punishment won’t come. The test might be in how you use the time you’re given.

Ayah 112

قَـٰلَ رَبِّ ٱحْكُم بِٱلْحَقِّ ۗ وَرَبُّنَا ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنُ ٱلْمُسْتَعَانُ عَلَىٰ مَا تَصِفُونَ

“Qāla rabbihkum bil haqq” - he said, my Master, judge with truth, make Your decision with justice. This is the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ speaking, and it’s a powerful moment. He has just been described as a mercy to all the worlds, yet here he’s reaching a point where even his immense patience is being tested.

After years of rejection, mockery, persecution and stubborn refusal, the Prophet ﷺ is saying: O Allah, they’ve reached the end of the line. Please pass Your verdict against them with justice. But notice what follows immediately.

“Wa rabbunār rahmānul musta’ān ’alā mā tasifūn” - and our Master is Ar-Rahman (the Exceedingly Merciful), the only One whose help can be sought against all these false descriptions you make of Him.

Even though the Prophet ﷺ has asked for judgment, Allah ﷻ identifies Himself as Ar-Rahman. The message is clear: even though My messenger has reached his limit, I am still the Exceedingly Merciful. I will wait. I won’t pass immediate judgment. My mercy precedes My wrath.

“Al-musta’ān” - the only One whose help can be sought. Against all the false attributions, the lies about Allah ﷻ, the fabrications about His nature and His message, the only source of help and support is Allah ﷻ Himself. Only He can give strength to endure these falsehoods and ultimately establish the truth.

The surah ends with this delicate balance: the Prophet’s ﷺ request has been noted and acknowledged (“qāla” - he said, implying Allah ﷻ heard him), but Allah’s ﷻ mercy still prevails. The warning has been delivered, the message has been conveyed, and now everyone should be careful, because even the mercy of Allah ﷻ has its limits.
